## Ischemic Stroke: FAST Criteria & Acute Management

### Definition
Ischemic stroke: Sudden-onset focal neurological deficit due to cerebrovascular occlusion causing infarction. Accounts for ~87% of all strokes.

### FAST Screening Criteria
| Sign | Assessment |
|------|------------|
| **F** - Face | Ask patient to smile; observe for unilateral facial droop |
| **A** - Arms | Ask patient to raise both arms; observe for unilateral drift or weakness |
| **S** - Speech | Ask patient to repeat a phrase; listen for slurring, aphasia |
| **T** - Time | Record exact time of symptom onset; call emergency services immediately |

### Extended BE-FAST Criteria
- **B**: Balance loss (sudden)
- **E**: Eyes (sudden vision loss, diplopia)

### Penumbra Concept
Ischemic core: Irreversibly infarcted tissue (CBF <10 mL/100g/min). Penumbra: Salvageable tissue surrounding core (CBF 10–20 mL/100g/min). Target of acute reperfusion therapy.

### tPA Eligibility Window
| Parameter | Threshold |
|-----------|----------|
| Symptom onset to treatment | ≤4.5 hours |
| Age | ≥18 years |
| BP before tPA | <185/110 mmHg |
| Blood glucose | 50–400 mg/dL |
| INR (if on warfarin) | ≤1.7 |
| Platelet count | ≥100,000/μL |

### Alteplase Dosing
- 0.9 mg/kg IV (max 90 mg)
- 10% bolus over 1 min, remainder over 60 min

### Red Flags (Absolute tPA Contraindications)
- Active intracranial hemorrhage on CT
- Prior intracranial hemorrhage history
- Ischemic stroke within 3 months
- Active internal bleeding
- BP >185/110 mmHg refractory to treatment
- Platelet count <100,000/μL
- Recent intracranial/spinal surgery within 3 months

## Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS)

### Definition
Standardized neurological assessment tool evaluating level of consciousness via three components. Range: 3 (deepest coma/death) to 15 (fully alert).

### Scoring Table
| Component | Response | Score |
|-----------|----------|-------|
| **Eye Opening (E)** | Spontaneous | 4 |
| | To verbal command | 3 |
| | To pain | 2 |
| | None | 1 |
| **Verbal Response (V)** | Oriented | 5 |
| | Confused | 4 |
| | Inappropriate words | 3 |
| | Incomprehensible sounds | 2 |
| | None | 1 |
| **Motor Response (M)** | Obeys commands | 6 |
| | Localizes pain | 5 |
| | Withdraws from pain | 4 |
| | Abnormal flexion (Decorticate) | 3 |
| | Abnormal extension (Decerebrate) | 2 |
| | None | 1 |

### Interpretation
| GCS Total | Severity |
|-----------|----------|
| 14–15 | Minor TBI |
| 9–13 | Moderate TBI |
| ≤8 | Severe TBI — intubation indicated |
| 3 | No function; brain death evaluation warranted |

### Red Flags
- GCS ≤8: Airway protection lost; immediate RSI intubation required
- GCS drop ≥2 points: Urgent repeat CT head; suspect herniation
- Asymmetric pupils + GCS decline: Uncal herniation until proven otherwise
- Decorticate → Decerebrate posturing: Brainstem compromise

## Seizure Classification & Management

### ILAE 2017 Classification

#### Focal Onset Seizures
- **Aware**: Consciousness preserved (formerly 'simple partial')
- **Impaired Awareness**: Consciousness altered (formerly 'complex partial')
- **Focal to Bilateral Tonic-Clonic**: Spreads to both hemispheres

#### Generalized Onset Seizures
| Type | Features |
|------|----------|
| Tonic-Clonic | Stiffening (tonic) → rhythmic jerking (clonic), 1–3 min |
| Absence | Brief (5–30 sec) behavioral arrest; 3 Hz spike-wave on EEG |
| Myoclonic | Brief (<1 sec) muscle jerks |
| Atonic | Sudden loss of muscle tone; drop attacks |
| Tonic | Sustained muscle stiffening |
| Clonic | Repetitive rhythmic jerking |

### Status Epilepticus Definition
Continuous seizure activity ≥5 minutes OR ≥2 discrete seizures without recovery of consciousness.

### Treatment Protocol
| Time | Intervention |
|------|-------------|
| 0–5 min | Benzodiazepine: Lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg IV (max 4 mg) |
| 5–20 min | Second-line: Levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV, Valproate 40 mg/kg IV, or Fosphenytoin 20 PE/kg IV |
| 20–40 min | Refractory: Intubation + Midazolam infusion or Propofol |
| >40 min | Super-refractory: Ketamine, Pentobarbital coma |

### Red Flags
- First seizure in adult >60 years: Structural/vascular cause until proven otherwise
- New focal seizure with fever: Meningoencephalitis or abscess
- Seizure + papilledema: Mass lesion with raised ICP
- Status epilepticus >30 min: Risk of permanent neuronal injury

## Asthma: GINA Guidelines Classification & Management

### Definition
Chronic heterogeneous airway disease characterized by airway hyperresponsiveness, reversible airflow obstruction, and type 2 eosinophilic inflammation.

### Severity Classification (GINA 2023)
| Parameter | Mild Intermittent | Mild Persistent | Moderate Persistent | Severe Persistent |
|-----------|------------------|-----------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Daytime symptoms | <2/week | >2/week, not daily | Daily | Continuous |
| Nighttime | ≤2/month | >2/month | >1/week | Frequent |
| FEV1 (% predicted) | ≥80% | ≥80% | 60–80% | <60% |
| SABA use | <2 days/week | >2 days/week | Daily | Several/day |
| GINA Step | Step 1 | Step 2 | Step 3–4 | Step 5 |

### GINA Step Therapy
| Step | Preferred Controller | Reliever |
|------|---------------------|----------|
| 1 | Low-dose ICS-formoterol PRN | ICS-formoterol |
| 2 | Low-dose ICS daily | SABA PRN |
| 3 | Low-dose ICS-LABA | SABA PRN |
| 4 | Medium-dose ICS-LABA | SABA PRN |
| 5 | High-dose ICS-LABA + biologics (anti-IL-5, anti-IL-4R) | SABA PRN |

### Acute Exacerbation: Severity Assessment
| Feature | Mild-Moderate | Severe | Life-Threatening |
|---------|---------------|--------|------------------|
| SpO2 | ≥92% | 90–92% | <90% |
| Speech | Sentences | Phrases | Words only |
| PEFR | 50–70% predicted | <50% predicted | <33% predicted |
| RR | <25/min | ≥25/min | Bradypnea |
| Accessory muscles | No | Yes | Paradoxical movement |

### Red Flags (Life-Threatening Asthma)
- Silent chest (absent wheeze despite distress): Near-fatal asthma
- Bradycardia + hypotension: Cardiovascular compromise
- Altered consciousness: Hypercapnic respiratory failure
- PEFR <33% predicted: Near-fatal threshold
- PaCO2 >45 mmHg: Ventilatory failure

## COPD: GOLD Criteria & Management

### Definition
Progressive airflow limitation due to abnormal airway/alveolar response to noxious particles. Post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC <0.70 confirms obstruction.

### GOLD Airflow Limitation Grades
| GOLD Grade | FEV1 (% predicted) | Severity |
|------------|-------------------|----------|
| GOLD 1 | ≥80% | Mild |
| GOLD 2 | 50–79% | Moderate |
| GOLD 3 | 30–49% | Severe |
| GOLD 4 | <30% | Very Severe |

### ABCD Assessment Tool (Symptoms + Exacerbations)
| Group | mMRC/CAT | Exacerbations/year |
|-------|----------|-------------------|
| A | mMRC 0–1 / CAT <10 | 0–1 (no hospitalization) |
| B | mMRC ≥2 / CAT ≥10 | 0–1 (no hospitalization) |
| E | Any | ≥2 or ≥1 hospitalization |

### Pharmacological Management
| Group | Initial Therapy |
|-------|----------------|
| A | SAMA or SABA |
| B | LAMA or LABA |
| E | LAMA + LABA (add ICS if eos ≥300 cells/μL) |

### Acute Exacerbation Treatment
- SABA + SAMA nebulization
- Prednisolone 30–40 mg PO ×5 days
- Antibiotics: Amoxicillin/Azithromycin (purulent sputum or CRP >10 mg/L)
- Controlled O2: Target SpO2 88–92%
- NIV (BiPAP) if pH <7.35 and PaCO2 >45 mmHg

### Red Flags
- pH <7.25: High NIV failure risk; prepare for intubation
- New RV failure (JVP↑, ankle edema, RV strain on ECG): Cor pulmonale
- PaCO2 >80 mmHg: Hypercapnic narcosis
- SpO2 <85% on 60% FiO2: Concurrent pneumonia or PE

## Community-Acquired Pneumonia: CURB-65 Severity Score

### Definition
Acute lower respiratory tract infection with new radiological infiltrate in a non-hospitalized patient or within 48h of admission.

### CURB-65 Scoring
| Parameter | Criterion | Score |
|-----------|-----------|-------|
| **C** - Confusion | New disorientation (person, place, time) | 1 |
| **U** - Urea | BUN >19 mg/dL (>7 mmol/L) | 1 |
| **R** - Respiratory Rate | ≥30 breaths/min | 1 |
| **B** - Blood Pressure | Systolic <90 mmHg OR Diastolic ≤60 mmHg | 1 |
| **65** - Age | ≥65 years | 1 |

### Risk Stratification & Disposition
| CURB-65 | 30-day Mortality | Management |
|---------|-----------------|------------|
| 0–1 | <3% | Outpatient PO antibiotics |
| 2 | ~9% | Consider hospitalization |
| 3–4 | ~15–22% | Inpatient IV antibiotics |
| 5 | ~57% | ICU, assess for mechanical ventilation |

### Empirical Antibiotic Regimens
| Setting | Regimen |
|---------|--------|
| Outpatient, no comorbidities | Amoxicillin 500 mg TID ×5 days |
| Outpatient, comorbidities | Amoxicillin-clavulanate + Azithromycin |
| Hospitalized, non-ICU | Beta-lactam + Macrolide OR Respiratory Fluoroquinolone |
| ICU | Beta-lactam + Azithromycin + Antipseudomonal (if risk factors) |

### Red Flags (ICU Admission Criteria — ATS/IDSA Minor)
- RR ≥30/min
- PaO2/FiO2 ratio ≤250
- Multilobar infiltrates
- Confusion/disorientation
- BUN ≥20 mg/dL
- WBC <4,000 cells/μL
- Platelet <100,000/μL
- Temperature <36°C
- Hypotension requiring aggressive fluid resuscitation
(≥3 minor criteria = ICU admission)

## Anaphylaxis: Recognition & Emergency Treatment Protocol

### Definition
Severe, systemic hypersensitivity reaction; typically IgE-mediated mast cell/basophil degranulation causing multisystem involvement. Onset: seconds to minutes.

### Diagnostic Criteria (WAO 2020)
Anaphylaxis is highly likely when ANY ONE of the following criteria is met:

**Criterion 1:** Acute illness with skin/mucosal involvement + at least one of:
- Respiratory compromise
- Hypotension or end-organ dysfunction

**Criterion 2:** Two or more of the following after allergen exposure:
- Skin/mucosal involvement
- Respiratory compromise
- Hypotension
- Persistent GI symptoms

**Criterion 3:** Hypotension alone after known allergen exposure
- Adults: SBP <90 mmHg or >30% decrease from baseline

### Treatment Protocol (Time-Critical)
| Priority | Intervention | Dose |
|----------|-------------|------|
| **1st** | Epinephrine IM | 0.3–0.5 mg IM (lateral thigh); 0.01 mg/kg in children (max 0.5 mg) |
| **2nd** | Supine position | Legs elevated unless respiratory compromise |
| **3rd** | O2 supplementation | High-flow 10–15 L/min via non-rebreather |
| **4th** | IV access + NS bolus | 1–2 L IV (adults); 20 mL/kg IV (children) |
| **5th** | H1 antihistamine | Diphenhydramine 25–50 mg IV |
| **6th** | Corticosteroid | Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV (does NOT replace epinephrine) |
| **7th** | H2 blocker | Ranitidine 50 mg IV (adjunct) |

### Epinephrine Repeat Dosing
Repeat IM epinephrine every 5–15 minutes if no improvement. IV epinephrine infusion (1–4 mcg/min) for refractory shock.

### Red Flags
- Delayed epinephrine administration: Single most preventable cause of anaphylaxis death
- Biphasic reaction: 1–72h after initial resolution; observe ≥4h minimum post-reaction
- Beta-blocker use: Epinephrine resistance; use glucagon 1–5 mg IV
- Tryptase >11.4 μg/L at 1–2h: Confirms mast cell activation

## Sepsis: qSOFA Criteria & Surviving Sepsis Campaign

### Definitions (Sepsis-3, 2016)
- **Sepsis**: Life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by dysregulated host response to infection. SOFA score ≥2 points above baseline.
- **Septic Shock**: Sepsis + vasopressor requirement to maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg + serum lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation. In-hospital mortality >40%.

### qSOFA (Quick SOFA) — Bedside Screen
| Criterion | Score |
|-----------|-------|
| Respiratory Rate ≥22/min | 1 |
| Altered Mental Status (GCS <15) | 1 |
| Systolic BP ≤100 mmHg | 1 |

qSOFA ≥2: High risk of poor outcome; initiate full SOFA assessment and sepsis workup.

### SOFA Score (Full)
| System | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|--------|---|---|---|---|---|
| Respiration (PaO2/FiO2) | ≥400 | 300–399 | 200–299 | 100–199 on ventilation | <100 on ventilation |
| Coagulation (Platelets ×10³/μL) | ≥150 | 100–149 | 50–99 | 20–49 | <20 |
| Liver (Bilirubin mg/dL) | <1.2 | 1.2–1.9 | 2.0–5.9 | 6.0–11.9 | >12.0 |
| Cardiovascular (MAP/Vasopressors) | MAP ≥70 | MAP <70 | Dopamine <5 | Dopamine 5–15 | Dopamine >15 |
| CNS (GCS) | 15 | 13–14 | 10–12 | 6–9 | <6 |
| Renal (Creatinine mg/dL) | <1.2 | 1.2–1.9 | 2.0–3.4 | 3.5–4.9 | >5.0 |

### Hour-1 Bundle (SSC 2018)
1. Measure lactate; repeat if >2 mmol/L
2. Blood cultures ×2 before antibiotics
3. Broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour
4. 30 mL/kg IV crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥4 mmol/L
5. Vasopressors (Norepinephrine 1st line) if MAP <65 mmHg

### Red Flags
- Lactate ≥4 mmol/L: Tissue hypoperfusion; associated with >28% mortality
- Fluid-refractory shock: Escalate vasopressors; add vasopressin 0.03 units/min
- New AKI + thrombocytopenia + elevated bilirubin: MODS (Multi-Organ Dysfunction Syndrome)

## Myocardial Infarction: STEMI vs NSTEMI Protocols

### Classification
| Type | ECG Finding | Mechanism | Priority |
|------|-------------|-----------|----------|
| **STEMI** | ST-elevation ≥1 mm (≥2 mm V1–V2) in ≥2 contiguous leads | Complete coronary occlusion | Emergent PCI |
| **NSTEMI** | ST-depression, T-wave inversion, or normal ECG + elevated troponin | Partial occlusion / plaque erosion | Urgent PCI (within 24h) |
| **Unstable Angina** | Dynamic ECG changes, no troponin rise | Plaque disruption, no necrosis | Early invasive strategy |

### STEMI Reperfusion Strategy
| Setting | Target Time | Strategy |
|---------|-------------|----------|
| PCI-capable center | Door-to-balloon ≤90 min | Primary PCI |
| Non-PCI center | FMC-to-device ≤120 min | Transfer for PCI |
| PCI not achievable ≤120 min | Door-to-needle ≤30 min | Fibrinolysis (tPA/Tenecteplase) |

### STEMI MONA Protocol (Initial)
- **M**: Morphine 2–4 mg IV for refractory pain (use cautiously)
- **O**: Oxygen only if SpO2 <90%
- **N**: Nitroglycerin SL 0.4 mg q5min ×3 (contraindicated if SBP <90 mmHg, RV infarct, PDE5 inhibitor use within 24h)
- **A**: Aspirin 300 mg PO loading dose

### Dual Antiplatelet Therapy (DAPT)
- Aspirin 75–100 mg + Ticagrelor 180 mg loading OR Clopidogrel 600 mg loading
- Duration: 12 months minimum post-PCI stenting

### NSTEMI Risk Stratification (GRACE Score)
| Risk Category | GRACE Score | 6-Month Mortality | PCI Timing |
|---------------|-------------|-------------------|------------|
| Low | <109 | <3% | Elective (>72h) |
| Intermediate | 109–140 | 3–8% | Early (<24h) |
| High | >140 | >8% | Immediate (<2h) |

### Red Flags
- New LBBB + chest pain: Treat as STEMI equivalent
- SBP <90 mmHg + STEMI: Cardiogenic shock; IABP/Impella support
- Posterior MI (ST depression V1–V4): Obtain posterior leads V7–V9
- RV infarct (ST elevation in V4R): Avoid nitrates and diuretics

## Statins: Mechanism, Contraindications & Toxicity

### Mechanism of Action
Statins competitively inhibit HMG-CoA reductase, the rate-limiting enzyme in the mevalonate pathway for cholesterol biosynthesis in hepatocytes. This reduces intracellular cholesterol → upregulates hepatic LDL receptors → increased LDL clearance from plasma.

### Drug Class Overview
| Statin | Lipophilicity | LDL Reduction | CYP Metabolism |
|--------|--------------|---------------|----------------|
| Atorvastatin | Lipophilic | 37–51% | CYP3A4 |
| Rosuvastatin | Hydrophilic | 45–63% | CYP2C9 (minor) |
| Simvastatin | Lipophilic | 28–41% | CYP3A4 |
| Pravastatin | Hydrophilic | 23–34% | Non-CYP |
| Fluvastatin | Lipophilic | 22–36% | CYP2C9 |

### Pleiotropic Effects (Beyond LDL Lowering)
- Anti-inflammatory: Reduces CRP and IL-6
- Endothelial: Improves nitric oxide bioavailability
- Plaque stabilization: Reduces lipid core volume

### Contraindications
- Active hepatic disease or unexplained persistent transaminase elevation (>3× ULN)
- Pregnancy and lactation (Category X)
- Concomitant strong CYP3A4 inhibitors with Simvastatin/Lovastatin (azole antifungals, protease inhibitors, macrolides)

### Toxicity Profile
| Adverse Effect | Incidence | Mechanism | Management |
|---------------|-----------|-----------|------------|
| Myalgia (CK normal) | 5–10% | Mitochondrial dysfunction | Reduce dose or switch |
| Myopathy (CK >10× ULN) | 0.1% | Impaired CoQ10/mevalonate | Discontinue immediately |
| Rhabdomyolysis (CK >10,000 IU/L) | <0.01% | Myocyte necrosis → myoglobinuria | Discontinue; aggressive IV hydration |
| Hepatotoxicity (transaminases >3× ULN) | <1% | Direct hepatocellular injury | Discontinue |
| New-onset Diabetes | ~10% relative risk increase | Reduced insulin sensitivity | Risk-benefit assessment |

### Red Flags
- CK >10,000 IU/L + dark urine: Rhabdomyolysis; risk of acute tubular necrosis
- Transaminases >3× ULN on two occasions: Discontinue statin
- Simvastatin >40 mg + Amlodipine: FDA warning; myopathy risk
- Simvastatin + Gemfibrozil: Contraindicated combination

## Anticoagulants: Mechanism, Monitoring & Reversal

### Drug Class Comparison
| Agent | Target | Monitoring | Reversal Agent |
|-------|--------|------------|----------------|
| Warfarin | Vitamin K-dependent factors (II, VII, IX, X, Protein C/S) | PT/INR | Vitamin K, 4-Factor PCC, FFP |
| Unfractionated Heparin (UFH) | Antithrombin → inhibits IIa + Xa | aPTT (1.5–2.5× normal) | Protamine sulfate |
| Low Molecular Weight Heparin (LMWH) | Antithrombin → inhibits Xa | Anti-Xa level (if required) | Protamine (60% effective) |
| Dabigatran | Direct thrombin inhibitor (Factor IIa) | Thrombin time | Idarucizumab (Praxbind) |
| Rivaroxaban/Apixaban | Direct Factor Xa inhibitor | Anti-Xa (if required) | Andexanet alfa |

### Warfarin INR Therapeutic Ranges
| Indication | Target INR |
|------------|------------|
| DVT/PE treatment | 2.0–3.0 |
| AF (non-valvular) | 2.0–3.0 |
| Mechanical aortic valve | 2.0–3.0 |
| Mechanical mitral valve | 2.5–3.5 |
| Recurrent VTE | 2.0–3.0 |

### Warfarin Drug Interactions (CYP2C9/VKORC1)
- **Potentiators** (increase INR): Amiodarone, Fluconazole, Metronidazole, NSAIDs, Ciprofloxacin
- **Inhibitors** (decrease INR): Rifampicin, Carbamazepine, St. John's Wort, Vitamin K-rich foods

### Reversal Protocols
| Scenario | Action |
|----------|--------|
| INR 4–9, no bleeding | Hold warfarin 1–2 doses; monitor |
| INR >9, no bleeding | Vitamin K 2.5–5 mg PO |
| Life-threatening bleed | 4-Factor PCC 25–50 IU/kg IV + Vitamin K 10 mg IV |
| Dabigatran major bleed | Idarucizumab 5 g IV |
| Rivaroxaban/Apixaban major bleed | Andexanet alfa (weight/dose-based bolus + infusion) |

### Red Flags
- INR >10: High intracranial hemorrhage risk; urgent reversal
- Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia (HIT): Platelets ↓>50% + thrombosis on heparin; stop all heparin immediately; use Argatroban or Danaparoid
- Warfarin in first trimester: Warfarin embryopathy (nasal hypoplasia, stippled epiphyses)

## Antibiotics: Mechanism, Spectrum & Toxicity

### Major Class Summary
| Class | Mechanism | Spectrum | Key Toxicity |
|-------|-----------|----------|-------------|
| Penicillins | Cell wall synthesis inhibition (PBP binding) | Gram+, some Gram- | Hypersensitivity, C. difficile |
| Cephalosporins (Gen 1–5) | Cell wall synthesis inhibition | Broad (generation-dependent) | Hypersensitivity (cross-reactive ~1–2%) |
| Carbapenems | Broad-spectrum beta-lactam | Gram+, Gram-, anaerobes | Seizures (Imipenem), C. difficile |
| Aminoglycosides | 30S ribosomal inhibition (bactericidal) | Gram- (incl. Pseudomonas) | Nephrotoxicity, Ototoxicity |
| Fluoroquinolones | DNA gyrase/topoisomerase IV inhibition | Gram-, atypicals | QTc prolongation, tendinopathy, C. difficile |
| Vancomycin | Cell wall synthesis (glycopeptide) | MRSA, Gram+ | Red Man Syndrome, nephrotoxicity |
| Macrolides | 50S ribosomal inhibition (bacteriostatic) | Atypicals, Gram+ | QTc prolongation, hepatotoxicity |
| Metronidazole | DNA strand breakage | Anaerobes, protozoa | Peripheral neuropathy, disulfiram reaction |
| Linezolid | 50S ribosomal inhibition | MRSA, VRE | Myelosuppression, serotonin syndrome |

### Beta-Lactam Allergy Cross-Reactivity
- Penicillin IgE-mediated allergy → cephalosporin cross-reactivity ~1–2% (lower than historically reported 10%)
- Carbapenems: <1% cross-reactivity with true penicillin allergy
- Same R1 side chain = higher cross-reactivity risk

### Aminoglycoside Monitoring
| Parameter | Conventional Dosing | Extended-Interval Dosing |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| Dosing frequency | Q8h | Q24h |
| Trough target (Gentamicin) | <2 mg/L | Undetectable at 18–24h |
| Peak target | 5–10 mg/L (Gram-) | N/A |
| Renal monitoring | Creatinine Q48h | Creatinine Q48h |

### Red Flags
- Aminoglycosides + loop diuretics: Synergistic ototoxicity; audiometry if prolonged use
- Vancomycin trough >20 mg/L: Nephrotoxicity risk; target AUC/MIC 400–600 per ASHP 2020 guidelines
- Fluoroquinolones in myasthenia gravis: May precipitate respiratory crisis
- Clindamycin/Cephalosporins with prolonged use: C. difficile colitis; test with stool PCR

## Pediatric Developmental Milestones (Age-Specific)

### Gross Motor Milestones
| Age | Expected Milestone |
|-----|-------------------|
| 2 months | Lifts head 45° in prone position |
| 4 months | Head control; rolls prone to supine |
| 6 months | Sits with support; bears weight on legs |
| 9 months | Sits without support; crawls |
| 12 months | Pulls to stand; cruises furniture; may take first steps |
| 15 months | Walks independently |
| 18 months | Runs stiffly; climbs stairs with help |
| 24 months | Runs well; walks up/down stairs (two feet per step) |
| 36 months | Climbs stairs alternating feet; rides tricycle |

### Fine Motor Milestones
| Age | Expected Milestone |
|-----|-------------------|
| 2 months | Hands open, tracks past midline |
| 4 months | Reaches for objects, bilateral grasp |
| 6 months | Transfers objects hand to hand |
| 9 months | Pincer grasp (inferior) |
| 12 months | Mature pincer grasp |
| 18 months | Stacks 3 blocks; scribbles spontaneously |
| 24 months | Stacks 6 blocks; copies circle |
| 36 months | Copies circle; uses scissors |

### Language Milestones
| Age | Expressive | Receptive |
|-----|------------|----------|
| 2 months | Cooing | Startles to sound |
| 6 months | Babbles (consonants) | Responds to name |
| 12 months | 1–3 words (mama, dada specific) | Follows 1-step commands |
| 18 months | 10–25 words | Points to body parts |
| 24 months | 2-word phrases; 50+ words | Follows 2-step commands |
| 36 months | 3-word sentences; 75% intelligibility | Follows 3-step commands |

### Social/Emotional Milestones
| Age | Milestone |
|-----|-----------|
| 2 months | Social smile |
| 6 months | Stranger anxiety begins |
| 9 months | Separation anxiety peak; object permanence |
| 18 months | Parallel play |
| 36 months | Cooperative play; takes turns |

### Red Flags (Developmental Regression)
- Loss of ANY previously acquired skill: Urgent developmental and neurological evaluation
- No babbling by 12 months: Audiology + speech-language referral
- No words by 16 months: Autism spectrum disorder screening (M-CHAT-R)
- No 2-word phrases by 24 months: Comprehensive developmental evaluation
- Persistent toe-walking >2 years: Assess for autism, cerebral palsy, muscular dystrophy

## Neonatal Emergency Vitals & Resuscitation

### Normal Neonatal Vital Signs
| Parameter | Term Neonate (0–28 days) | Preterm (<37 weeks) |
|-----------|-------------------------|--------------------|
| Heart Rate | 120–160 bpm | 120–170 bpm |
| Respiratory Rate | 40–60 breaths/min | 40–60 breaths/min |
| Systolic BP | 60–80 mmHg | 45–60 mmHg |
| Temperature (axillary) | 36.5–37.5°C | 36.5–37.5°C |
| SpO2 (1–10 min post-birth) | 85–95% (transitional) | — |
| SpO2 (>10 min post-birth) | ≥95% | ≥95% |
| Blood Glucose | ≥2.6 mmol/L (≥47 mg/dL) | ≥2.6 mmol/L |

### APGAR Score (Assessed at 1 and 5 minutes)
| Sign | 0 | 1 | 2 |
|------|---|---|---|
| **A**ppearance | Blue/pale all over | Body pink, extremities blue | Completely pink |
| **P**ulse | Absent | <100 bpm | ≥100 bpm |
| **G**rimace | No response | Grimace | Cry or cough |
| **A**ctivity | Limp | Some flexion | Active motion |
| **R**espiration | Absent | Weak/irregular | Strong cry |

| Score | Interpretation |
|-------|----------------|
| 7–10 | Normal |
| 4–6 | Moderate depression; stimulation + O2 |
| 0–3 | Severe depression; immediate resuscitation |

### NRP Algorithm (Neonatal Resuscitation Program)
1. Warm, dry, stimulate → assess breathing + HR
2. If RR inadequate or HR <100 bpm: PPV at 40–60/min (21% O2 for term; 21–30% for preterm)
3. If HR <60 bpm after 30 sec PPV: Chest compressions (3:1 ratio with PPV); increase FiO2 to 100%
4. If HR <60 bpm after compressions: Epinephrine 0.01–0.03 mg/kg IV (umbilical vein)
5. Consider: Volume expansion (NS 10 mL/kg) if hypovolemia suspected

### Critical Neonatal Emergencies
| Condition | Key Finding | Immediate Action |
|-----------|-------------|------------------|
| Congenital diaphragmatic hernia | Scaphoid abdomen, bowel sounds in chest | Intubate; avoid BMV |
| Tension pneumothorax | Sudden deterioration, shifted heart sounds | Needle decompression |
| Hypoglycemia (<47 mg/dL) | Jitteriness, apnea, seizures | 10% Dextrose 2 mL/kg IV bolus |
| Transposition of Great Arteries | Profound cyanosis, SpO2 unresponsive to O2 | Prostaglandin E1 (PGE1) infusion |

### Red Flags
- APGAR 0–3 at 5 minutes: High risk of hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy; initiate therapeutic hypothermia if eligible
- Persistent cyanosis unresponsive to 100% O2: Structural cardiac defect; urgent echocardiography
- Abdominal distension + bilious vomiting in neonate: Malrotation with volvulus until proven otherwise
- Temperature <36°C on admission: Hypothermia worsens hypoglycemia, acidosis, coagulopathy

## Heart Failure: Classification, Pathophysiology & Management

### Definition
Heart failure (HF): Clinical syndrome of inadequate cardiac output relative to metabolic demands, OR adequate output only at elevated filling pressures. Classified by ejection fraction:

| Type | EF | Mechanism |
|------|----|-----------|
| HFrEF (HF with reduced EF) | <40% | Systolic dysfunction |
| HFmrEF (mildly reduced EF) | 40–49% | Mixed |
| HFpEF (preserved EF) | ≥50% | Diastolic dysfunction |

### NYHA Functional Classification
| Class | Symptoms |
|-------|----------|
| I | No limitation; ordinary activity asymptomatic |
| II | Slight limitation; comfortable at rest, symptoms with moderate exertion |
| III | Marked limitation; comfortable at rest, symptoms with minimal exertion |
| IV | Symptoms at rest; unable to carry out any activity |

### Biomarkers
| Marker | Threshold | Interpretation |
|--------|-----------|----------------|
| BNP | ≥100 pg/mL | High sensitivity for HF |
| NT-proBNP | ≥300 pg/mL (acute); ≥125 pg/mL (chronic) | More stable; longer half-life |
| BNP <35 pg/mL | Rule-out HF | High negative predictive value |

### Evidence-Based Pharmacotherapy (HFrEF)
| Drug Class | Example | Mortality Benefit |
|------------|---------|-------------------|
| ACE Inhibitor / ARB | Ramipril, Valsartan | 16–27% RRR (SOLVD, CHARM) |
| Beta-Blocker | Carvedilol, Bisoprolol | 34% RRR (MERIT-HF, COPERNICUS) |
| MRA | Spironolactone, Eplerenone | 30% RRR (RALES, EMPHASIS-HF) |
| SGLT2 Inhibitor | Dapagliflozin, Empagliflozin | 25% RRR (DAPA-HF, EMPEROR) |
| ARNI | Sacubitril/Valsartan | 20% RRR over ACEi (PARADIGM-HF) |

### Red Flags (Acute Decompensated Heart Failure)
- SpO2 <90% on room air: High-flow O2/NIV; consider intubation
- SBP <90 mmHg + signs of hypoperfusion: Cardiogenic shock; inotropes + mechanical support
- BNP >1000 pg/mL with minimal diuresis: IV loop diuretic dose escalation or renal replacement
- Hyponatremia <130 mEq/L in HF: Severe; associated with poor prognosis

## Atrial Fibrillation: Management Framework

### Definition
AF: Supraventricular tachyarrhythmia with uncoordinated atrial electrical activity; absent P waves + irregularly irregular RR intervals on ECG. Most common sustained cardiac arrhythmia.

### Classification
| Type | Duration |
|------|----------|
| First detected | Single episode, regardless of duration |
| Paroxysmal | Terminates spontaneously or with intervention <7 days |
| Persistent | >7 days; requires intervention to terminate |
| Long-standing persistent | ≥1 year; rhythm control still pursued |
| Permanent | Accepted ongoing AF; rhythm control abandoned |

### Stroke Risk: CHA₂DS₂-VASc Score
| Factor | Score |
|--------|-------|
| CHF or LVEF <40% | 1 |
| Hypertension | 1 |
| Age ≥75 years | 2 |
| Diabetes mellitus | 1 |
| Stroke/TIA history | 2 |
| Vascular disease (MI, PAD, aortic plaque) | 1 |
| Age 65–74 years | 1 |
| Sex category (female) | 1 |

| Score | Annual Stroke Risk | Anticoagulation |
|-------|------------------|-----------------|
| 0 (male), 1 (female) | Low (<1%) | No anticoagulation |
| 1 (male) | Intermediate | Consider anticoagulation |
| ≥2 | High | Anticoagulate (DOAC preferred over warfarin) |

### Rate Control Targets
- Lenient: Resting HR <110 bpm (acceptable in most)
- Strict: Resting HR <80 bpm (symptomatic patients)
- Agents: Beta-blockers (1st line), Non-DHP CCBs (Diltiazem/Verapamil), Digoxin (adjunct)

### Cardioversion Criteria
- AF <48h duration: May cardiovert without TEE
- AF ≥48h or unknown duration: Anticoagulate ≥3 weeks before cardioversion OR TEE to exclude LAA thrombus
- Post-cardioversion: Anticoagulate ≥4 weeks regardless of CHA₂DS₂-VASc

### Red Flags
- AF with pre-excitation (WPW): Avoid AV nodal agents (adenosine, digoxin, verapamil); can precipitate VF
- AF + hemodynamic instability: Urgent electrical cardioversion (synchronized DC)
- Rapid ventricular response >150 bpm in HF: IV Amiodarone or Digoxin (avoid rate-limiting CCBs)

## Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA): Diagnostic Criteria & Management

### Diagnostic Criteria (ADA)
| Parameter | Mild DKA | Moderate DKA | Severe DKA |
|-----------|----------|--------------|------------|
| Blood Glucose | >250 mg/dL | >250 mg/dL | >250 mg/dL |
| Arterial pH | 7.25–7.30 | 7.00–7.24 | <7.00 |
| Serum Bicarbonate | 15–18 mEq/L | 10–14 mEq/L | <10 mEq/L |
| Urine/Serum Ketones | Positive | Positive | Positive |
| Anion Gap | >10 | >12 | >12 |
| Mental Status | Alert | Alert/Drowsy | Stupor/Coma |

### Anion Gap Calculation
Anion Gap = Na⁺ − (Cl⁻ + HCO₃⁻); Normal: 8–12 mEq/L

### Pathophysiology
Insulin deficiency → counterregulatory hormones ↑ (glucagon, cortisol, catecholamines) → lipolysis → free fatty acids → hepatic ketogenesis (beta-hydroxybutyrate, acetoacetate) → metabolic acidosis + osmotic diuresis.

### DKA Management Protocol

#### Fluids
- NS 1 L/h × 1–2h, then 250–500 mL/h based on hydration status
- Switch to 0.45% NaCl when Na corrected; add dextrose when glucose <250 mg/dL

#### Insulin
- Regular insulin 0.1 units/kg IV bolus (optional), then 0.1 units/kg/h infusion
- Reduce to 0.05 units/kg/h when glucose <250 mg/dL
- Continue insulin infusion until anion gap closes AND pH >7.30

#### Potassium Replacement
| Serum K⁺ | Action |
|----------|--------|
| <3.3 mEq/L | Hold insulin; replace K⁺ at 40 mEq/h before starting insulin |
| 3.3–5.0 mEq/L | Add 20–40 mEq K⁺ to each liter IV |
| >5.0 mEq/L | Hold K⁺; check every 2h |

#### Bicarbonate
Not routinely recommended; consider if pH <6.9 (50 mEq NaHCO₃ in 200 mL NS over 2h)

### Red Flags
- Cerebral edema: Headache + altered consciousness during treatment (especially in pediatric DKA); CT head; Mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg IV
- Persistent anion gap despite glucose normalization: Incomplete resolution; continue insulin drip
- Hypokalemia after insulin initiation: Cardiac arrhythmia risk; continuous cardiac monitoring
- Triglycerides >1000 mg/dL in DKA: Hypertriglyceridemia-induced pancreatitis as precipitant

## Hypoglycemia: Classification, Recognition & Treatment

### Definition (ADA Glucose Thresholds)
| Level | Blood Glucose | Clinical Significance |
|-------|--------------|----------------------|
| Level 1 | 54–70 mg/dL (3.0–3.9 mmol/L) | Alert value; treatment recommended |
| Level 2 | <54 mg/dL (<3.0 mmol/L) | Clinically significant hypoglycemia |
| Level 3 | Severe cognitive impairment, requires assistance | Any glucose level |

### Symptom Categories
#### Adrenergic (Sympathoadrenal — Early Warning)
- Diaphoresis, palpitations, tremor, anxiety, pallor, tachycardia

#### Neuroglycopenic (CNS glucose deprivation — Late/Severe)
- Confusion, seizure, focal neurological deficits, coma, death

### Treatment Protocol
| Severity | Patient Status | Treatment |
|----------|---------------|----------|
| Mild-Moderate | Conscious + able to swallow | 15g fast-acting carbohydrates PO (4 glucose tablets, 150 mL juice) |
| Moderate | Confused but responsive | Oral glucose gel (buccal) |
| Severe | Unconscious / unable to swallow | Dextrose 50% (D50W) 25–50 mL IV bolus OR Glucagon 1 mg IM/SC |
| IV access unavailable | Any | Glucagon 1 mg IM; Nasal Glucagon 3 mg (Baqsimi) |

### Rule of 15
15g carbohydrates → recheck glucose in 15 minutes → repeat if glucose still <70 mg/dL → once normalized, eat a meal or snack.

### Common Causes
| Category | Causes |
|----------|--------|
| Medication | Insulin, Sulfonylureas, Meglitinides |
| Medical | Adrenal insufficiency, Hepatic failure, Insulinoma |
| Factitious | Exogenous insulin/sulfonylurea |
| Post-surgical | Gastric bypass (late dumping) |

### Red Flags
- Prolonged hypoglycemia despite treatment: Suspect Sulfonylurea overdose; admit for 24h glucose monitoring
- Recurrent severe hypoglycemia unawareness: Hypoglycemia-associated autonomic failure (HAAF)
- Hypoglycemia + hyponatremia + hyperkalemia: Addisonian crisis; cortisol replacement (Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV) immediately
- Fasting glucose <50 mg/dL in non-diabetic: Insulinoma workup (C-peptide, proinsulin)

## Acute Kidney Injury (AKI): KDIGO Staging & Management

### Definition (KDIGO 2012)
AKI: Increase in serum creatinine (SCr) ≥0.3 mg/dL within 48h OR ≥1.5× baseline within 7 days OR urine output (UO) <0.5 mL/kg/h for ≥6h.

### KDIGO Staging
| Stage | Serum Creatinine | Urine Output |
|-------|-----------------|-------------|
| 1 | ≥0.3 mg/dL increase OR 1.5–1.9× baseline | <0.5 mL/kg/h for 6–12h |
| 2 | 2.0–2.9× baseline | <0.5 mL/kg/h for ≥12h |
| 3 | ≥3× baseline OR ≥4.0 mg/dL OR initiation of RRT | <0.3 mL/kg/h for ≥24h or anuria ≥12h |

### Etiology Classification
| Category | Cause | FENa |
|----------|-------|------|
| Pre-renal | Hypovolemia, cardiogenic, hepatorenal | <1% |
| Intrinsic | ATN, glomerulonephritis, AIN, vasculitis | >2% |
| Post-renal | BPH, calculi, malignancy (bilateral obstruction) | Variable |

### FENa Formula
FENa (%) = (Urine Na × Plasma Cr) / (Plasma Na × Urine Cr) × 100
Note: FENa unreliable if diuretics given, contrast nephropathy, myoglobinuria.

### Management Principles
1. Identify and treat underlying cause
2. Optimize volume status: Avoid over-resuscitation; target MAP ≥65 mmHg
3. Avoid nephrotoxins: NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, iodinated contrast (hold metformin)
4. Electrolyte management:
   - Hyperkalemia: Calcium gluconate, insulin + dextrose, Kayexalate, dialysis
   - Hyponatremia: Fluid restriction if dilutional
5. Renal Replacement Therapy (RRT) Indications (AEIOU): Acidosis pH <7.1, Electrolyte emergency (K⁺ >6.5), Intoxication, Overload (refractory pulmonary edema), Uremia (BUN >100 mg/dL or uremic symptoms)

### Red Flags
- Anuria <50 mL/24h: Post-renal obstruction or cortical necrosis; urgent renal ultrasound
- AKI + diffuse alveolar hemorrhage: Goodpasture syndrome (Anti-GBM); emergency plasmapheresis
- AKI + purpuric rash + thrombocytopenia: TMA/HUS; ADAMTS13 and shiga toxin testing
- Creatinine doubling within 24h: Rapidly progressive; escalate to nephrology

## Pulmonary Embolism: Diagnosis & Risk Stratification

### Wells Score (Pre-Test Probability)
| Criterion | Points |
|-----------|--------|
| Clinical signs of DVT | 3 |
| PE #1 diagnosis OR no alternative diagnosis | 3 |
| HR >100 bpm | 1.5 |
| Immobilization ≥3 days or surgery in past 4 weeks | 1.5 |
| Prior DVT or PE | 1.5 |
| Hemoptysis | 1 |
| Malignancy (active, on treatment, or palliative) | 1 |

| Score | Probability | D-dimer Strategy |
|-------|-------------|------------------|
| ≤4 | Low–Intermediate | D-dimer; if negative → PE excluded |
| >4 | High | Proceed to CTPA directly |

### D-Dimer Interpretation
- Standard threshold: 500 ng/mL (FEU)
- Age-adjusted threshold: Age × 10 ng/mL for patients >50 years
- D-dimer elevated in: PE, DVT, DIC, pregnancy, malignancy, post-surgery (low specificity)

### PE Risk Stratification (ESC 2019)
| Class | Criteria | 30-day Mortality | Management |
|-------|---------|-----------------|------------|
| Massive (High-risk) | SBP <90 mmHg or drop >40 mmHg | >15% | Systemic thrombolysis or embolectomy |
| Submassive (Intermediate-high) | RV dysfunction + elevated troponin | 3–15% | Anticoagulation; monitor for deterioration |
| Low-risk | PESI class I–II, normal RV, negative troponin | <1% | Home anticoagulation; HESTIA criteria |

### Systemic Thrombolysis Protocol
- Alteplase 100 mg IV over 2h (10 mg bolus + 90 mg over 2h)
- Absolute contraindications: Recent (<3 months) intracranial surgery/trauma, prior ICH, active bleeding

### Red Flags
- Pulseless electrical activity (PEA) in PE: Immediate bedside echocardiography; consider thrombolysis in cardiac arrest
- Saddle PE on CTPA: High clot burden; discuss catheter-directed thrombolysis vs. surgical embolectomy
- Submassive PE + hemodynamic deterioration: Lower threshold for rescue thrombolysis
- CTPA with RV/LV diameter ratio >0.9: RV pressure overload; troponin + BNP for risk stratification

## Upper GI Bleeding: Assessment & Management

### Definition
Any hemorrhage proximal to the ligament of Treitz. Presents as hematemesis, 'coffee ground' emesis, or melena.

### Glasgow-Blatchford Score (GBS) — Pre-Endoscopy Risk
| Parameter | Finding | Score |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| BUN (mg/dL) | 18–22 | 2 |
| | 22–28 | 3 |
| | 28–70 | 4 |
| | >70 | 6 |
| Hemoglobin (g/dL), Male | 12–13 | 1 |
| | 10–12 | 3 |
| | <10 | 6 |
| Hemoglobin (g/dL), Female | 10–12 | 1 |
| | <10 | 6 |
| SBP (mmHg) | 100–109 | 1 |
| | 90–99 | 2 |
| | <90 | 3 |
| HR ≥100 bpm | | 1 |
| Melena | | 1 |
| Syncope | | 2 |
| Hepatic disease | | 2 |
| Cardiac failure | | 2 |

| GBS | Risk | Intervention |
|-----|------|-------------|
| 0 | Very low | Outpatient management |
| 1–5 | Low | Inpatient; elective endoscopy |
| ≥6 | High | Urgent endoscopy <24h |

### Initial Resuscitation
- 2 large-bore IV lines (16G or larger)
- Type and crossmatch (2–6 units pRBC)
- Transfuse if Hb <7 g/dL (Hb <8 g/dL in cardiovascular disease)
- PPI: Pantoprazole 80 mg IV bolus + 8 mg/h infusion
- NPO; restrict to clears after stabilization

### Variceal Bleeding Protocol
1. Terlipressin 2 mg IV q4h (or Octreotide 50 mcg bolus + 50 mcg/h infusion)
2. Ceftriaxone 1 g IV daily (primary prophylaxis reduces mortality)
3. Urgent endoscopy with variceal band ligation within 12h
4. TIPS (Transjugular Intrahepatic Portosystemic Shunt) if refractory

### Red Flags
- Hematemesis with visible clot or fresh blood: High-risk stigmata; urgent endoscopy within 24h (within 6–12h if hemodynamically unstable)
- Bleeding + suspected aortic graft: Aortoenteric fistula; CT angiography + emergent surgical consultation
- SBP <90 mmHg + Hb <8 g/dL: Massive UGIB; massive transfusion protocol
- Rebleed after successful endoscopic therapy: Repeat endoscopy; consider surgery or angioembolization

## Toxicology: Toxidromes & Antidotes

### Major Toxidromes
| Toxidrome | Causative Agents | Key Signs | Antidote |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------|----------|
| Opioid | Heroin, Oxycodone, Fentanyl | Miosis, respiratory depression, CNS depression | Naloxone 0.4–2 mg IV/IM/IN |
| Cholinergic (muscarinic) | Organophosphates, Carbamates | Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, Bradycardia, Bronchospasm (SLUDGE) | Atropine + Pralidoxime |
| Anticholinergic | TCAs, Antihistamines, Atropine | Mydriasis, dry skin, hyperthermia, urinary retention, delirium | Physostigmine (selected cases) |
| Sympathomimetic | Cocaine, Amphetamines, MDMA | Mydriasis, hypertension, tachycardia, hyperthermia | Benzodiazepines |
| Serotonin Syndrome | SSRIs, SNRIs, MAOIs | Clonus, hyperthermia, agitation, diaphoresis | Cyproheptadine; Cooling |
| Sedative-Hypnotic | Benzodiazepines, Barbiturates | CNS depression, normal pupils, respiratory depression | Flumazenil (benzodiazepines only — use with caution) |

### Acetaminophen (APAP) Toxicity
| Stage | Time Post-Ingestion | Features |
|-------|---------------------|----------|
| I | 0–24h | Nausea, vomiting, malaise |
| II | 24–72h | RUQ pain, elevated LFTs, AKI begins |
| III | 72–96h | Peak hepatotoxicity; ALT/AST >1000 IU/L, coagulopathy |
| IV | 4 days–2 weeks | Recovery or fulminant hepatic failure |

#### Rumack-Matthew Nomogram
- Plot serum APAP level vs. time post-ingestion
- Possible toxicity line: APAP 150 mcg/mL at 4h; 4.6 mcg/mL at 24h
- Initiate NAC if APAP level above nomogram line

#### N-Acetylcysteine (NAC) Protocol
- IV: 150 mg/kg over 1h → 50 mg/kg over 4h → 100 mg/kg over 16h
- PO: 140 mg/kg loading → 70 mg/kg q4h × 17 doses

### General Decontamination
| Method | Indication | Timing |
|--------|------------|--------|
| Activated charcoal | Oral ingestion, intact airway | <1–2h post-ingestion |
| Gastric lavage | Massive ingestion, recent (<1h) | Selected cases only |
| Whole bowel irrigation | Iron, sustained-release drugs, body packers | GoLYTELY 1.5–2 L/h |

### Red Flags
- Opioid with apnea: Immediate bag-mask ventilation before Naloxone
- Naloxone after long-acting opioid (methadone, fentanyl patches): Repeat dosing or infusion (2/3 effective bolus dose per hour)
- INR >6 in APAP toxicity: Fulminant hepatic failure; liver transplant evaluation
- QRS >100 ms in TCA overdose: Sodium bicarbonate 1–2 mEq/kg IV bolus for QRS narrowing

## Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC): Diagnosis & Management

### Definition
Acquired syndrome of systemic activation of coagulation leading to intravascular fibrin formation, consumption of clotting factors and platelets, and simultaneous thrombosis and hemorrhage.

### ISTH Overt DIC Score
| Parameter | Finding | Score |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| Platelet count (×10³/μL) | >100 | 0 |
| | 50–100 | 1 |
| | <50 | 2 |
| Fibrin-related markers (D-dimer) | No increase | 0 |
| | Moderate increase | 2 |
| | Strong increase (>5× ULN) | 3 |
| PT prolongation (seconds) | <3 | 0 |
| | 3–6 | 1 |
| | >6 | 2 |
| Fibrinogen (g/L) | ≥1.0 | 0 |
| | <1.0 | 1 |

Score ≥5: Compatible with overt DIC
Score <5: Suggestive but not overt; repeat in 24h

### Common Triggers
- Sepsis (most common)
- Trauma/Burns
- Obstetric: Abruptio placentae, AFE, HELLP syndrome
- Malignancy (especially AML-M3, mucin-secreting adenocarcinomas)
- Transfusion reactions
- Envenomation

### Management
| Component | Indication | Product |
|-----------|------------|--------|
| Plasma | Active bleeding + INR >1.5 | FFP 15–20 mL/kg |
| Platelets | Active bleeding + platelets <50×10³/μL | Platelets to target >50×10³/μL |
| Fibrinogen | Fibrinogen <1.5 g/L + bleeding | Cryoprecipitate 10 units |
| Heparin | Thrombosis-predominant DIC (purpura fulminans) | UFH infusion at low dose |
| Tranexamic acid | Fibrinolysis-dominant (trauma) | 1g IV over 10 min within 3h |

### Red Flags
- Fibrinogen <0.5 g/L: Severe consumptive coagulopathy; aggressive cryoprecipitate replacement
- DIC + AML-M3 (APL): Initiating ATRA simultaneously with supportive transfusion reduces mortality
- Obstetric DIC (AFE/HELLP): Involve obstetric anesthesia + hematology; high maternal mortality
- Purpura fulminans in DIC: Protein C concentrate if available; high amputation risk

## Bacterial Meningitis: Diagnosis & Treatment

### Clinical Features
- Classic triad: Fever + Neck stiffness + Altered consciousness (present in <50%)
- Photophobia, phonophobia, headache, nausea/vomiting
- Kernig sign: Inability to extend knee when hip is flexed 90°
- Brudzinski sign: Involuntary hip/knee flexion on neck flexion
- Petechial/purpuric rash: Meningococcal meningitis until proven otherwise

### CSF Analysis: Bacterial vs. Viral
| Parameter | Bacterial | Viral | Tuberculous | Fungal |
|-----------|-----------|-------|-------------|--------|
| Opening pressure | >200 mmH₂O | Normal–elevated | Elevated | Elevated |
| Appearance | Cloudy | Clear | Clear–cloudy | Clear–cloudy |
| WBC/μL | 1000–10,000 (PMN) | 100–1000 (lymph) | 100–500 (lymph) | 50–500 (lymph) |
| Protein (mg/dL) | >100 | 50–100 | 100–500 | 100–500 |
| Glucose | <40 (low) | Normal | Low (<45) | Low |
| CSF/Serum Glucose | <0.3 | >0.6 | <0.5 | <0.3 |

### Empirical Antibiotic Regimens (by Age)
| Age Group | Pathogen Coverage | Regimen |
|-----------|-----------------|--------|
| Neonate (<1 month) | GBS, E. coli, Listeria | Ampicillin + Cefotaxime |
| 1–3 months | GBS, E. coli, Listeria, N. meningitidis | Ampicillin + Ceftriaxone |
| 3 months–50 years | N. meningitidis, S. pneumoniae | Ceftriaxone + Vancomycin |
| >50 years | S. pneumoniae, Listeria | Ceftriaxone + Vancomycin + Ampicillin |
| Immunocompromised | As above + Cryptococcus | + Liposomal Amphotericin B |

### Dexamethasone Adjunctive Therapy
- 0.15 mg/kg IV q6h × 4 days
- Administer 15–30 min BEFORE or with first antibiotic dose
- Benefit: Reduces hearing loss in pneumococcal meningitis; reduces mortality
- Do NOT give if: Cryptococcal meningitis (worsens outcomes)

### Red Flags
- Do NOT delay antibiotics for LP if: GCS <13, papilledema, focal neurology, seizure, immunocompromised, new rash
- Blood cultures + antibiotics FIRST, then CT, then LP
- Petechial rash + fever ± hypotension: Meningococcemia — Penicillin G or Ceftriaxone immediately (before LP)
- CSF opening pressure >400 mmH₂O: Drain CSF slowly; fungal meningitis most likely

## Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE): SLICC Diagnostic Criteria

### Definition
SLE: Systemic autoimmune disease characterized by immune dysregulation, autoantibody production, and multi-organ inflammation. F:M ratio ~9:1; peak onset ages 15–40.

### SLICC 2012 Classification Criteria
Requires ≥4 criteria (at least 1 clinical + 1 immunological) OR biopsy-proven lupus nephritis + ANA or anti-dsDNA.

#### Clinical Criteria (11)
1. Acute cutaneous lupus (malar rash, photosensitive rash)
2. Chronic cutaneous lupus (discoid rash)
3. Oral ulcers (non-painful)
4. Non-scarring alopecia
5. Synovitis ≥2 joints
6. Serositis (pleuritis, pericarditis)
7. Renal: Urine protein/creatinine >500 mg/g OR RBC casts
8. Neurological (seizures, psychosis, mononeuritis multiplex, peripheral neuropathy)
9. Hemolytic anemia
10. Leukopenia (<4000/μL) or Lymphopenia (<1000/μL)
11. Thrombocytopenia (<100,000/μL)

#### Immunological Criteria (6)
1. ANA above laboratory reference range
2. Anti-dsDNA above reference (2× ELISA)
3. Anti-Sm (highly specific for SLE)
4. Antiphospholipid antibodies (lupus anticoagulant, anti-β2GPI, false-positive VDRL)
5. Low complement (C3, C4, or CH50)
6. Direct Coombs test positive (without hemolytic anemia)

### Antibody Specificity Reference
| Antibody | Sensitivity | Specificity | Association |
|----------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| ANA | 98% | 74% | Screening test |
| Anti-dsDNA | 70% | 90% | Disease activity/nephritis |
| Anti-Sm | 25% | 99% | Highly specific for SLE |
| Anti-Ro/SSA | 30–40% | — | Neonatal lupus, Sjögren overlap |
| Anti-phospholipid | 30–40% | — | Thrombosis, recurrent miscarriage |

### Lupus Nephritis ISN/RPS Classification
| Class | Description | Treatment |
|-------|-------------|----------|
| I | Minimal mesangial | Monitoring |
| II | Mesangial proliferative | Low-dose immunosuppression |
| III/IV | Focal/Diffuse proliferative (most severe) | High-dose steroids + MMF or Cyclophosphamide |
| V | Membranous | MMF + steroids |
| VI | Sclerosing (>90% sclerotic glomeruli) | Renal replacement planning |

### Red Flags
- Anti-dsDNA rising + C3/C4 falling: Lupus nephritis flare; renal biopsy
- Pleuritis + fever + pericardial effusion: Serositis or SLE-related infection; exclude
- New seizure in SLE without other cause: Neuropsychiatric lupus; MRI + LP
- Thrombocytopenia <20,000/μL: Risk of spontaneous hemorrhage; IVIG or steroids

## Acute Pancreatitis: Severity Assessment & Management

### Definition
Acute pancreatic inflammation caused by premature activation of digestive enzymes. Most common causes: gallstones (40%), alcohol (30%), hypertriglyceridemia, drugs, post-ERCP.

### Diagnostic Criteria (≥2 of 3 features)
1. Epigastric pain radiating to back
2. Serum lipase or amylase ≥3× upper limit of normal
3. Imaging findings of pancreatitis (CT/MRI)

### BISAP Score (Bedside Index of Severity in Acute Pancreatitis)
| Parameter | Criterion | Score |
|-----------|-----------|-------|
| BUN | >25 mg/dL | 1 |
| Impaired Mental Status | GCS <15 | 1 |
| SIRS | ≥2 SIRS criteria | 1 |
| Age | >60 years | 1 |
| Pleural effusion | Present on imaging | 1 |

| BISAP | In-hospital Mortality |
|-------|----------------------|
| 0–2 | <2% |
| 3 | 5–8% |
| 4–5 | >15% |

### Revised Atlanta Classification (2012)
| Category | Criteria |
|----------|----------|
| Mild | No organ failure; no local complications |
| Moderately Severe | Transient organ failure (<48h) OR local complications |
| Severe | Persistent organ failure (>48h) |

### Management
1. Aggressive IV hydration: Lactated Ringer's 250–500 mL/h (preferred over NS; reduces SIRS)
2. Analgesia: IV opioids (Morphine, Hydromorphone); avoid NSAIDs
3. Nil by mouth initially; early enteral nutrition (NG or NJ route within 24–48h if severe)
4. Antibiotics: NOT routinely indicated; reserve for infected necrosis (CT-guided FNA if suspected)
5. ERCP: Only if concurrent cholangitis or CBD obstruction (not routine for gallstone pancreatitis)
6. Cholecystectomy: Same admission if mild gallstone pancreatitis

### Red Flags
- Lipase >10× ULN + SIRS criteria: High risk for severe pancreatitis; ICU monitoring
- Pancreatic necrosis >30% on CT with fever: Infected necrosis; CT-guided aspiration for culture
- Hypocalcemia <8 mg/dL in pancreatitis: Fat saponification; severe case
- Triglycerides >1000 mg/dL: Hypertriglyceridemia-induced pancreatitis; insulin infusion + plasmapheresis
- Cullen's / Grey Turner's sign (periumbilical / flank ecchymosis): Hemorrhagic pancreatitis; high mortality

## Serotonin Syndrome vs. Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome

### Serotonin Syndrome
#### Mechanism
Excess serotonergic activity at 5-HT1A and 5-HT2A receptors; typically from drug-drug interactions.

#### Hunter Criteria (Diagnosis)
Serotonergic agent exposure + ONE of:
- Clonus (spontaneous, inducible, or ocular) + agitation/diaphoresis
- Tremor + hyperreflexia
- Hypertonia + temperature >38°C + clonus

#### Common Drug Combinations
- SSRI + MAOI (highest risk; potentially fatal)
- SSRI + Tramadol
- SSRI + Linezolid
- SSRI + Methylene blue
- Triptans + SSRIs

#### Management
1. Discontinue all serotonergic agents
2. Benzodiazepines for agitation and seizures
3. Cyproheptadine 12 mg PO loading (5-HT2A antagonist), then 2 mg q2h
4. Active cooling for temperature >41°C
5. Intubation + paralysis if severe rigidity/hyperthermia

### Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS)
#### Mechanism
Dopamine receptor blockade (antipsychotics) causing hypodopaminergic state in striatum and hypothalamus.

#### DSM-5 Diagnostic Features
1. Antipsychotic/dopamine-blocking agent exposure
2. Hyperthermia (>38°C on two occasions)
3. Generalized lead-pipe muscle rigidity
4. Altered mental status
5. Autonomic instability (diaphoresis, labile BP, tachycardia)
6. Elevated CK (often >1000 IU/L; may exceed 100,000 IU/L)
7. No other causative agent

#### Management
1. Discontinue antipsychotic/causative agent immediately
2. Aggressive IV hydration
3. Dantrolene 1–2.5 mg/kg IV (reduces rigidity)
4. Bromocriptine 2.5–5 mg TID/QID PO (dopamine agonist)
5. ICU admission; cool blankets; monitor for rhabdomyolysis/AKI

### Key Differentiators
| Feature | Serotonin Syndrome | NMS |
|---------|-------------------|-----|
| Onset | Hours | Days |
| Muscle | Hyperreflexia, clonus | Lead-pipe rigidity |
| Pupils | Mydriasis | Variable |
| Bowel sounds | Hyperactive | Decreased |
| Cause | Serotonergic drugs | Antipsychotics |

### Red Flags
- Temperature >41°C: Risk of rhabdomyolysis + DIC + MODS
- CK >10,000 IU/L: Rhabdomyolysis + AKI risk; aggressive IV hydration
- Hemodynamic instability in NMS: ICU; avoid dopamine-blocking antiemetics

## Stevens-Johnson Syndrome (SJS) & Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis (TEN)

### Definition & Classification
Mucocutaneous drug hypersensitivity with keratinocyte apoptosis and epidermal detachment.
| Diagnosis | BSA Detachment |
|-----------|---------------|
| SJS | <10% |
| SJS/TEN overlap | 10–30% |
| TEN | >30% |

### Common Causative Drugs
- Allopurinol (most common worldwide)
- Carbamazepine, Lamotrigine, Phenytoin
- Sulfonamides (sulfamethoxazole)
- Nevirapine
- NSAIDs (Oxicam class)

### SCORTEN Mortality Score (Score each criterion = 1 point)
| Criterion | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Age | >40 years |
| Malignancy | Present |
| Heart rate | >120 bpm |
| BUN | >28 mg/dL |
| Detached BSA | >10% on Day 1 |
| Bicarbonate | <20 mEq/L |
| Blood glucose | >252 mg/dL |

| SCORTEN | Predicted Mortality |
|---------|--------------------|
| 0–1 | 3.2% |
| 2 | 12.1% |
| 3 | 35.3% |
| 4 | 58.3% |
| ≥5 | >90% |

### Management
1. Immediate discontinuation of causative drug
2. Transfer to burn unit (BSA >10%) or ICU
3. Fluid resuscitation: 2–4 mL/kg/%BSA involved
4. Non-adherent wound dressings (biological membranes, Biobrane)
5. Eye care: Ophthalmology consult; artificial tears + topical antibiotics
6. Oral care: Chlorhexidine mouth washes
7. Immunomodulatory agents: Cyclosporine 3–5 mg/kg/day (most evidence) OR Etanercept
8. Avoid: Systemic steroids (controversial; may increase infection risk)

### Red Flags
- BSA >30% (TEN): Burns unit admission; mortality >30–50%
- Ocular involvement: Acute ocular involvement → chronic conjunctival scarring; ophthalmology within 24h
- SCORTEN ≥3 on admission: High mortality; intensive supportive care
- Sepsis in TEN: Leading cause of death; low threshold for blood cultures and empirical antibiotics

## Preeclampsia & Eclampsia: Diagnosis & Management

### Definitions
- **Preeclampsia**: New-onset hypertension (≥140/90 mmHg on two occasions ≥4h apart) after 20 weeks gestation + proteinuria (≥300 mg/24h or PCR ≥30 mg/mmol) OR severe features
- **Eclampsia**: New-onset grand-mal seizure in woman with preeclampsia
- **HELLP Syndrome**: Hemolysis + Elevated Liver enzymes + Low Platelets

### Severe Features of Preeclampsia
| Feature | Threshold |
|---------|----------|
| SBP | ≥160 mmHg on two occasions |
| DBP | ≥110 mmHg on two occasions |
| Thrombocytopenia | Platelets <100,000/μL |
| Renal insufficiency | Creatinine >1.1 mg/dL or 2× baseline |
| Impaired liver function | Transaminases ≥2× ULN + severe RUQ pain |
| Pulmonary edema | New |
| New-onset headache | Unresponsive to medication |

### Antihypertensive Agents in Pregnancy
| Agent | Dose | Notes |
|-------|------|-------|
| Labetalol | 20–80 mg IV bolus, or 200 mg PO | 1st line IV; avoid in asthma |
| Hydralazine | 5–10 mg IV q20 min | Reflex tachycardia common |
| Nifedipine (CCB) | 10–20 mg PO immediate-release | 1st line oral; avoid SL route |
| Methyldopa | 250–500 mg TID PO | Safe; for chronic management |

Target BP: 130–150/80–100 mmHg (avoid overcorrection)

### Magnesium Sulfate (Seizure Prophylaxis)
- Loading: MgSO4 4–6 g IV over 15–20 min
- Maintenance: 1–2 g/h IV infusion
- Therapeutic range: 4–7 mEq/L
- Toxicity monitoring: Deep tendon reflexes (loss at 7–10 mEq/L), RR (depression at >12 mEq/L), UO
- Antidote: Calcium gluconate 1 g IV over 10 min

### HELLP Syndrome Diagnostic Criteria
| Parameter | Threshold |
|-----------|----------|
| Hemolysis | LDH >600 IU/L; abnormal blood film |
| Elevated liver enzymes | AST/ALT >70 IU/L |
| Low platelets | <100,000/μL |

### Red Flags
- Eclamptic seizure: MgSO4 bolus 2–4 g IV over 5 min; maintain airway; deliver fetus after stabilization
- Platelets <50,000/μL in HELLP: Delivery regardless of gestational age; transfuse if active bleeding
- Pulmonary edema in preeclampsia: Furosemide; strict fluid restriction (fluid overload risk high)
- RUQ pain + rising transaminases: Hepatic capsule distension; risk of subcapsular hematoma rupture

## Acute Compartment Syndrome: Recognition & Management

### Definition
Elevated pressure within a closed fascial compartment exceeding perfusion pressure, leading to ischemia and necrosis of muscle and nerves within that compartment.

### Classical Presentation (6 Ps)
| Sign | Clinical Finding |
|------|------------------|
| **P**ain | Severe, out of proportion to injury; with passive stretch |
| **P**ressure | Compartment feels tense/woody |
| **P**aresthesia | Numbness/tingling in distribution of compressed nerves |
| **P**aralysis | Late finding; indicates irreversible ischemia |
| **P**allor | Skin appears pale/mottled |
| **P**ulselessness | Very late finding; pulses often preserved early |

### Compartment Pressure Measurement
- Normal intracompartmental pressure: 0–8 mmHg
- Delta pressure = Diastolic BP − Compartment pressure
- **Delta pressure <30 mmHg: Indication for fasciotomy**
- Absolute compartment pressure >30 mmHg: Indication for fasciotomy

### Common Sites & At-Risk Injuries
| Site | Precipitating Injury |
|------|---------------------|
| Anterior leg | Tibial fracture, crush injury |
| Volar forearm | Supracondylar fracture, IV infiltration |
| Hand | Crush injury, high-pressure injection |
| Gluteal/thigh | Femoral fracture, prolonged compression |
| Foot | Calcaneal fracture |

### Management
1. Remove all circumferential dressings and casts immediately
2. Elevate limb to heart level (not above — reduces perfusion)
3. Maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg (optimize perfusion pressure)
4. **Fasciotomy within 6 hours of diagnosis** (all compartments)
5. Post-fasciotomy: Daily wound inspection; delayed primary closure at 48–72h or split-thickness skin graft

### Red Flags
- Pain with passive stretch is PATHOGNOMONIC: Diagnosis is clinical; do not delay for pressure measurement
- Delay >6h to fasciotomy: Irreversible Volkmann ischemic contracture
- Fasciotomy wounds with black/necrotic muscle: Debridement required; risk of life-threatening sepsis
- AKI post-compartment syndrome: Rhabdomyolysis + myoglobinuria; aggressive IV hydration

## Thyroid Storm: Diagnosis & Emergency Management

### Definition
Life-threatening exacerbation of hyperthyroidism with multisystem dysfunction; mortality 10–30% even with treatment.

### Burch-Wartofsky Point Scale (BWPS)
| Parameter | Finding | Points |
|-----------|---------|--------|
| Temperature (°C) | 37.2–37.7 | 5 |
| | 37.8–38.2 | 10 |
| | 38.3–38.8 | 15 |
| | 38.9–39.4 | 20 |
| | 39.5–39.9 | 25 |
| | ≥40.0 | 30 |
| CNS effects | Absent | 0 |
| | Mild (agitation) | 10 |
| | Moderate (delirium/psychosis) | 20 |
| | Severe (seizures/coma) | 30 |
| GI-hepatic | Absent | 0 |
| | Moderate (diarrhea/nausea) | 10 |
| | Severe (jaundice) | 20 |
| Heart Rate (bpm) | 99–109 | 5 |
| | 110–119 | 10 |
| | 120–129 | 15 |
| | 130–139 | 20 |
| | ≥140 | 25 |
| AF | Present | 10 |
| Precipitating event | Present | 10 |

| BWPS | Diagnosis |
|------|----------|
| ≥45 | Thyroid storm |
| 25–44 | Impending storm |
| <25 | Unlikely |

### Treatment Protocol (BBTIPS)
| Step | Drug | Mechanism |
|------|------|-----------|
| **B** - Block synthesis | PTU 200 mg q4h PO/NG OR Methimazole 20 mg q4h | Inhibit thyroid peroxidase |
| **B** - Block release | Lugol's iodine (5 drops q6h) — give 1h AFTER PTU | Wolff-Chaikoff effect |
| **T** - Treat hyperstimulation | Cholestyramine 4g QID (reduces enterohepatic recirculation) | Bile acid sequestrant |
| **I** - Inhibit peripheral conversion | PTU (preferred over methimazole) OR Propranolol | Reduces T4→T3 conversion |
| **P** - Propranolol | 60–80 mg q4h PO or 1–2 mg IV | Heart rate control |
| **S** - Steroids | Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV q8h | Blocks peripheral T4→T3; treats relative adrenal insufficiency |

### Red Flags
- Agitation + temperature >40°C + tachycardia >140 bpm: BWPS ≥45; initiate full protocol
- Cardiac failure in thyroid storm: Amiodarone controversial (contains iodine); preferred: Esmolol IV
- Precipitant search mandatory: Infection, surgery, trauma, radioiodine, contrast dye, DKA
- PTU in first trimester: Switch to methimazole in 2nd/3rd trimester (hepatotoxicity risk with PTU)

## Raised Intracranial Pressure (ICP): Recognition & Management

### Normal ICP Values
- Adults: 7–15 mmHg
- Children: 3–7 mmHg
- Neonates: 1.5–6 mmHg
- Therapeutic target in TBI: ICP <22 mmHg (BTF Guidelines 2016)

### Cerebral Perfusion Pressure (CPP)
CPP = MAP − ICP
- Target in TBI: 60–70 mmHg
- CPP <50 mmHg: Critical ischemia threshold

### Monro-Kellie Doctrine
Fixed skull volume = Brain (80%) + CSF (10%) + Blood (10%). Increase in one compartment must be compensated by another; decompensation → exponential ICP rise.

### Herniation Syndromes
| Syndrome | Mechanism | Clinical Features |
|----------|-----------|------------------|
| Uncal (transtentorial) | Temporal lobe herniation through tentorium | Ipsilateral CN III palsy (dilated pupil), contralateral hemiplegia |
| Central | Downward displacement of brainstem | Bilateral Babinski, decorticate→decerebrate |
| Tonsillar | Cerebellar tonsils through foramen magnum | Bradycardia, respiratory arrest (Cushing reflex) |
| Subfalcine | Cingulate gyrus under falx | ACA territory infarction, leg weakness |

### Cushing Reflex (Triad)
- Hypertension (widened pulse pressure)
- Bradycardia
- Irregular/slow respirations
(Represents terminal herniation with brainstem compression)

### Emergency ICP-Lowering Measures
| Intervention | Target | Notes |
|-------------|--------|-------|
| Head elevation | 30° | Improves venous drainage |
| Hyperventilation | PaCO2 35–40 mmHg (acute: 30–35) | Transient cerebral vasoconstriction; short-term bridge only |
| Osmotherapy: Mannitol | 0.25–1.0 g/kg IV bolus | Target serum osmolality <320 mOsm/kg |
| Osmotherapy: HTS (3% NaCl) | 250 mL bolus | Target Na 145–155 mEq/L |
| Sedation + analgesia | Target ICP <22 mmHg | Propofol, Fentanyl |
| Decompressive craniectomy | Refractory ICP >25 mmHg | Surgical option |

### Red Flags
- Fixed, dilated pupil (blown pupil): Uncal herniation; immediate CT, neurosurgical consult
- Cushing triad: Imminent brainstem herniation; immediate osmotherapy + neurosurgery
- Papilledema in LP workup: Perform CT first; LP contraindicated until mass lesion excluded

## Tension Pneumothorax: Recognition & Emergency Decompression

### Definition
Air accumulates under pressure in the pleural space (one-way valve mechanism), causing ipsilateral lung collapse + contralateral mediastinal shift + impaired venous return → cardiovascular collapse.

### Clinical Features
| Sign | Finding |
|------|---------|
| Tracheal deviation | Contralateral (away from tension) |
| Absent breath sounds | Ipsilateral |
| Distended neck veins | Present (unless concomitant hypovolemia) |
| Hypotension | Present (obstructive shock) |
| Tachycardia | Present |
| Cyanosis | Late sign |

### Diagnosis is CLINICAL — Do NOT wait for CXR if hemodynamically unstable.

### Emergency Decompression
#### Needle Thoracostomy (Immediate)
- Site: 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line OR 4th–5th ICS, anterior axillary line
- Technique: 14–16G angiocath inserted ABOVE rib (avoids neurovascular bundle)
- Rush of air confirms diagnosis
- Temporizing measure only; follow with chest tube

#### Chest Tube (Definitive)
- Site: 4th–5th ICS, anterior axillary line (safe triangle)
- Size: 28–32 Fr for pneumothorax; 32–36 Fr for hemothorax
- Connect to underwater seal drainage; confirm re-expansion on CXR

### Simple vs. Tension Pneumothorax
| Feature | Simple | Tension |
|---------|--------|--------|
| Hemodynamics | Stable | Unstable |
| Tracheal deviation | No | Yes (late) |
| JVD | No | Yes |
| Treatment | Chest tube / aspiration | Immediate needle decompression |

### Red Flags
- PEA arrest post-trauma: Tension pneumothorax until proven otherwise; bilateral needle decompression
- Failed needle thoracostomy: Chest wall thickness >4.5 cm (in obese patients); advance longer needle or proceed directly to finger thoracostomy
- Air leak continuing >5 days post-chest tube: Bronchopleural fistula; thoracic surgery consult
- Hemothorax >1500 mL or >200 mL/h: Massive hemothorax; emergency thoracotomy

## Pediatric Febrile Seizures: Classification & Management

### Definition
Febrile seizure: Seizure in febrile child aged 6 months–5 years without evidence of intracranial infection or metabolic cause.

### Classification
| Type | Characteristics |
|------|----------------|
| **Simple** | Generalized, duration <15 min, single occurrence within 24h, complete recovery |
| **Complex** | Focal onset OR duration >15 min OR ≥2 within 24h OR incomplete recovery |

### Immediate Management
1. Lateral decubitus positioning (recovery position)
2. Airway, Breathing, Circulation assessment
3. Temperature measurement and fever management (Paracetamol 15 mg/kg OR Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg)
4. Blood glucose (bedside)

### Acute Seizure Termination (If >5 minutes)
| Step | Drug | Dose | Route |
|------|------|------|-------|
| 1st | Midazolam | 0.2–0.3 mg/kg | Buccal or intranasal |
| 1st (alt) | Diazepam | 0.3–0.5 mg/kg (max 10 mg) | PR |
| 2nd | Lorazepam | 0.1 mg/kg IV | IV (if access) |
| 3rd | Levetiracetam | 40–60 mg/kg IV | IV |
| 4th | Phenobarbitone | 20 mg/kg IV | IV |

### Lumbar Puncture Indications
- Age <12 months: Always consider LP (signs may be subtle)
- Age 12–18 months: LP if not fully immunized (HIB, pneumococcus) or clinical concern
- Age >18 months: LP only if clinical signs of meningitis (neck stiffness, petechiae, photophobia)

### Recurrence Risk
| Risk Factor | Prevalence | Risk of Recurrence |
|-------------|------------|-------------------|
| Family history of febrile seizures | 30% | 50% |
| Age <1 year at first seizure | | 50% |
| Lower temperature at seizure | | Higher recurrence |

### Red Flags
- Complex febrile seizure: Full septic workup + neurology consult
- Febrile seizure <6 months age: Meningitis/encephalitis until excluded
- Todd's palsy post-febrile seizure: Transient focal weakness (30–60 min); confirm resolution
- No fever found after reported febrile seizure: Consider Dravet syndrome (SCN1A mutation)

## Cardiac Tamponade: Recognition & Pericardiocentesis

### Definition
Pericardial effusion accumulates sufficient pressure to compress cardiac chambers, impairing filling and causing obstructive shock. Rate of accumulation is more critical than volume.

### Beck's Triad (Classic — seen in only 30–40% of cases)
1. Hypotension
2. Elevated JVP (distended neck veins)
3. Muffled heart sounds

### Additional Signs
- **Pulsus paradoxus**: Drop in SBP >10 mmHg with inspiration (abnormal normally <10 mmHg)
- **Kussmaul sign**: Paradoxical increase in JVP with inspiration (also seen in constrictive pericarditis)
- **Tachycardia**: Compensatory
- **Electrical alternans**: Alternating QRS amplitude on ECG (swinging heart motion)

### Echocardiographic Findings
- Right atrial collapse (early, in systole)
- Right ventricular collapse (specific — in diastole)
- IVC dilation with <50% respiratory collapse
- Respiratory variation in mitral valve inflow >25%

### Pericardiocentesis Technique
- Access: Subxiphoid approach (45° angle, aimed toward left shoulder) under echo guidance
- Needle: 18G spinal needle
- Position: 30–45° semirecumbent
- Confirm pericardial position: Echo visualization or pressure tracing (pericardial pressure = low, non-pulsatile)
- Drain: Aspirate until hemodynamic improvement; leave pigtail catheter for recurrent effusions

### Tamponade vs. Constrictive Pericarditis
| Feature | Tamponade | Constrictive Pericarditis |
|---------|-----------|---------------------------|
| Pulsus paradoxus | Present | Present (mild) |
| Kussmaul sign | Absent | Present |
| Effusion on echo | Present | Absent |
| Pericardial knock | Absent | Present (early S3) |
| Respiratory ventricular variation | >25% | Present |

### Red Flags
- Equalization of diastolic pressures in all 4 chambers: Pathognomonic for tamponade on cardiac catheterization
- Traumatic tamponade (hemopericardium): Do NOT perform pericardiocentesis alone; emergent pericardial window or thoracotomy
- PEA arrest + suspected tamponade: Blind subxiphoid pericardiocentesis before echo confirmation
- Rapidly re-accumulating effusion post-drainage: Malignancy, aortic dissection (contraindication to pericardiocentesis), tuberculosis

## HIV/AIDS: Staging, Opportunistic Infections & ART

### WHO Staging (CD4 Count Based)
| Stage | CD4 Count | Features |
|-------|-----------|----------|
| 1 (Asymptomatic) | ≥500 cells/μL | Persistent generalized lymphadenopathy |
| 2 (Mild) | 350–499 cells/μL | Minor weight loss, recurrent respiratory tract infections |
| 3 (Advanced) | 200–349 cells/μL | Oral candidiasis, unexplained fever, pulmonary TB |
| 4 (AIDS) | <200 cells/μL | AIDS-defining illnesses |

### Opportunistic Infections by CD4 Count
| CD4 (cells/μL) | Opportunistic Infection |
|----------------|------------------------|
| <500 | Kaposi sarcoma, Oral candidiasis |
| <350 | Esophageal candidiasis, Pulmonary TB |
| <200 | PCP (Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia), Toxoplasmosis |
| <100 | Cryptococcal meningitis, CMV retinitis |
| <50 | Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC), CMV disease |

### Prophylaxis Guidelines
| CD4 Count | Prophylaxis Agent | Indication |
|-----------|------------------|------------|
| <200 cells/μL | TMP-SMX DS 1 tab daily | PCP prophylaxis |
| <100 cells/μL | TMP-SMX DS 1 tab daily | Toxoplasmosis prophylaxis |
| <50 cells/μL | Azithromycin 1200 mg weekly | MAC prophylaxis |
| All | TMP-SMX (if CD4 <350) | Bacterial infections |  

### First-Line ART (WHO-Preferred Regimen 2023)
- Dolutegravir (INSTI) + Tenofovir alafenamide (NRTI) + Emtricitabine (NRTI)
- Alternative: Dolutegravir + Lamivudine (dual therapy)

### IRIS (Immune Reconstitution Inflammatory Syndrome)
Paradoxical worsening of infection after ART initiation; occurs as immune system recovers.
- Common trigger: TB, cryptococcosis, CMV
- Management: Continue ART; NSAID or Prednisone for severe IRIS

### Red Flags
- Cryptococcal meningitis: LP with high opening pressure; therapeutic drainage; Amphotericin B + Flucytosine induction
- PCP pneumonia + hypoxia: TMP-SMX high-dose + Prednisolone (if PaO2 <70 mmHg)
- CMV retinitis: Fundoscopy; IV Ganciclovir induction → Valganciclovir maintenance
- CD4 <50 without ART: MAC prophylaxis mandatory

## Corticosteroids: Mechanism, Equivalence & Adverse Effects

### Mechanism of Action
Glucocorticoids bind intracellular GR (glucocorticoid receptor) → nuclear translocation → transcription factor modulation:
- Anti-inflammatory: Inhibit NF-κB → ↓ IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α, COX-2
- Immunosuppressive: Reduce T-cell proliferation, lymphocyte trafficking
- Metabolic: Stimulate gluconeogenesis, lipolysis, protein catabolism

### Potency Equivalence Table
| Drug | Glucocorticoid Potency | Mineralocorticoid Potency | Duration |
|------|----------------------|--------------------------|----------|
| Hydrocortisone | 1 (reference) | 1 | Short (8–12h) |
| Prednisolone | 4 | 0.8 | Intermediate (12–36h) |
| Methylprednisolone | 5 | 0.5 | Intermediate (12–36h) |
| Dexamethasone | 25–30 | 0 | Long (36–54h) |
| Fludrocortisone | 10 | 125 | — (mineralocorticoid replacement) |
| Betamethasone | 25–30 | 0 | Long |

### Major Adverse Effects
| System | Effect |
|--------|--------|
| Metabolic | Hyperglycemia, weight gain, dyslipidemia |
| Musculoskeletal | Osteoporosis, avascular necrosis (femoral head), proximal myopathy |
| Endocrine | HPA axis suppression → adrenal insufficiency on withdrawal |
| Ophthalmologic | Posterior subcapsular cataracts, glaucoma |
| Dermatologic | Skin thinning, striae, impaired wound healing, purpura |
| Psychiatric | Euphoria, psychosis, insomnia (dose-dependent) |
| Immunologic | Susceptibility to opportunistic infections |
| Cardiovascular | Hypertension, fluid retention |
| GI | Peptic ulceration (especially with NSAIDs) |

### Steroid Tapering Principle
HPA suppression occurs with >20 mg prednisolone equivalent daily >3 weeks. Taper if prolonged course to avoid adrenal crisis.

### Red Flags
- Abrupt steroid cessation after >3 weeks: Adrenal crisis; Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV stat + 200 mg/24h infusion
- Steroids in uncontrolled DM: Hyperglycemic hyperosmolar state risk; frequent glucose monitoring
- Long-term steroids without bisphosphonate: Osteoporosis prophylaxis (Alendronate/Risedronate) required if >3 months therapy
- Psychosis on steroids: Reduce dose; Haloperidol for acute management

## Multiple Sclerosis: McDonald Criteria & Management

### Definition
MS: Chronic inflammatory demyelinating disease of the CNS characterized by lesions disseminated in space (DIS) and time (DIT), with relapsing-remitting or progressive course.

### McDonald 2017 Criteria
#### Diagnosis requires:
- ≥2 clinical attacks with objective evidence
- DIS: ≥1 T2 lesion in ≥2 regions (periventricular, cortical/juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord)
- DIT: Simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions OR new T2/enhancing lesion on follow-up
- CSF-specific oligoclonal bands (if DIS/DIT not met by imaging)

### Typical Presentations
| Syndrome | Features |
|----------|----------|
| Optic Neuritis | Unilateral painful visual loss; relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) |
| Internuclear Ophthalmoplegia | MLF lesion → ipsilateral adduction failure, contralateral nystagmus |
| Lhermitte Sign | Electric shock sensation down spine on neck flexion |
| Uhthoff Phenomenon | Symptom worsening with heat/fever (Uhthoff) |
| Transverse Myelitis | Incomplete spinal cord syndrome; sensory level |

### CSF Findings
| Finding | Significance |
|---------|--------------|
| Oligoclonal IgG bands (≥2 bands, not in serum) | Found in >95% MS patients |
| Elevated IgG index | Increased intrathecal IgG synthesis |
| Mild pleocytosis (<50 WBC/μL) | Non-specific |

### MRI Characteristics
- Periventricular: Dawson's fingers (perpendicular to corpus callosum)
- Infratentorial: Brainstem, cerebellar lesions
- Gadolinium-enhancing lesions: Active inflammation (blood-brain barrier disruption)
- T2/FLAIR hyperintensities: Old and new lesions

### Disease-Modifying Therapies (DMTs)
| Efficacy | Drug | Route |
|----------|------|-------|
| Low | Beta-interferons, Glatiramer | SC/IM injection |
| Moderate | Teriflunomide, Dimethyl fumarate | Oral |
| High | Natalizumab, Ocrelizumab, Cladribine, Alemtuzumab | IV/SC/Oral |

### Red Flags
- Rapid progressive MS: Consider high-efficacy DMT early
- Natalizumab + JC virus seropositive >24 months: PML (Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy) risk; monitor JC index
- Optic neuritis without MS treatment: 50% risk of MS within 15 years; preventive DMT in high-risk patients
- Spinal cord lesion >3 vertebral segments: Consider NMOSD (anti-AQP4 antibody) rather than MS

## Burns: Assessment & Initial Management

### Burn Depth Classification
| Degree | Depth | Appearance | Sensation | Healing |
|--------|-------|------------|-----------|--------|
| Superficial (1st) | Epidermis | Erythema, no blisters | Painful | 3–5 days |
| Superficial partial-thickness (2nd) | Epidermis + superficial dermis | Moist, blistered, red/pink | Very painful | 14 days |
| Deep partial-thickness (2nd deep) | Epidermis + deep dermis | Pale/mottled, may have blisters | Reduced sensation | >21 days; graft often needed |
| Full-thickness (3rd) | All skin layers | Dry, leathery, white/brown/black | Painless | Requires grafting |
| Deep full-thickness (4th) | Muscle/bone involved | Charred | No sensation | Amputation |

### TBSA Estimation
#### Rule of Nines (Adult)
| Region | TBSA |
|--------|------|
| Head + Neck | 9% |
| Each Arm | 9% |
| Anterior Trunk | 18% |
| Posterior Trunk | 18% |
| Each Leg | 18% |
| Perineum | 1% |

Note: Superficial (1st degree) burns NOT included in TBSA calculation.

### Parkland Formula (Fluid Resuscitation)
- 4 mL × body weight (kg) × %TBSA burned = 24h total Lactated Ringer's
- Give 50% in first 8h (from time of injury, not hospital arrival)
- Give remaining 50% over next 16h
- Target urine output: 0.5–1 mL/kg/h (adults); 1 mL/kg/h (children)

### Inhalation Injury Criteria
- Singed nasal hair or eyebrows
- Carbonaceous sputum
- Facial burns
- Hoarseness or stridor
- Enclosed space exposure
→ Suspected inhalation injury: Early intubation (airway edema worsens over hours)

### Burns Transfer Criteria (ABA Guidelines)
- Burns >10% TBSA (children); >20% TBSA (adults)
- Full-thickness burns >5% TBSA
- Burns involving face, hands, feet, genitalia, perineum, major joints
- Inhalation injury
- Chemical or electrical burns
- Burns with significant pre-existing medical conditions

### Red Flags
- CO poisoning (cherry-red skin, normal SpO2): SpO2 falsely normal; use ABG CO-oximetry; 100% FiO2 until COHb <5%
- Circumferential burns extremity/chest: Escharotomy required for compartment syndrome or restricted ventilation
- Burns + hypotension: Under-resuscitation or inhalation injury; reassess Parkland calculation
- Urine output >1 mL/kg/h: Over-resuscitation risk; reduce IV rate

## Beta-Blockers: Pharmacology, Indications & Contraindications

### Receptor Selectivity
| Drug | β1 Selectivity | β2 | α1 | ISA | Class |
|------|---------------|----|----|-----|-------|
| Metoprolol | Selective | — | — | No | Cardioselective |
| Atenolol | Selective | — | — | No | Cardioselective |
| Bisoprolol | Selective | — | — | No | Cardioselective |
| Carvedilol | Non-selective | + | Yes | No | Third-generation (α+β blocker) |
| Propranolol | Non-selective | + | — | No | Non-selective |
| Labetalol | Non-selective | + | Yes | — | α+β blocker |
| Pindolol | Non-selective | + | — | Yes | ISA (intrinsic sympathomimetic) |

### Pharmacodynamic Effects
- Negative chronotropy: ↓ HR (SA node automaticity)
- Negative inotropy: ↓ Contractility
- ↓ AV conduction: Slows PR interval
- ↓ Renin release: Antihypertensive mechanism
- ↓ Oxygen demand: Angina mechanism

### Indications
| Indication | Preferred Agent |
|------------|----------------|
| Heart failure (HFrEF) | Carvedilol, Bisoprolol, Metoprolol succinate |
| Hypertension | Any (but not first-line per JNC8) |
| Stable angina | Any |
| Post-MI | Metoprolol, Atenolol |
| AF rate control | Metoprolol |
| Varices prophylaxis (portal HTN) | Propranolol, Carvedilol |
| Thyroid storm | Propranolol (non-selective; blocks T4→T3) |
| Migraine prophylaxis | Propranolol, Metoprolol |

### Contraindications
- Decompensated heart failure with active fluid overload (relative)
- Cardiogenic shock
- Severe bradycardia (HR <50 bpm) or high-degree AV block
- Severe reactive airway disease (especially non-selective beta-blockers)
- Cocaine intoxication: Unopposed alpha-adrenergic stimulation → coronary spasm
- Peripheral arterial disease (caution; worsens claudication)

### Red Flags
- Abrupt beta-blocker withdrawal: Rebound tachycardia, angina, MI; taper over 1–2 weeks
- Non-selective beta-blocker in asthma: Risk of fatal bronchospasm; use cardioselective agent if mandatory
- Beta-blocker + verapamil IV: Life-threatening bradycardia and AV block
- Overdose: Glucagon 5–10 mg IV (bypasses beta-receptor); high-dose insulin euglycemic therapy

## Liver Cirrhosis: Severity Scoring & Complications

### Child-Pugh Score
| Parameter | 1 point | 2 points | 3 points |
|-----------|---------|----------|----------|
| Total Bilirubin (mg/dL) | <2 | 2–3 | >3 |
| Albumin (g/dL) | >3.5 | 2.8–3.5 | <2.8 |
| INR | <1.7 | 1.7–2.3 | >2.3 |
| Ascites | None | Mild | Moderate–Severe |
| Encephalopathy | None | Grade I–II | Grade III–IV |

| Class | Score | 1-Year Survival | 2-Year Survival |
