|-------|-------|----------------|----------------|
| A (Compensated) | 5–6 | 100% | 85% |
| B (Significant) | 7–9 | 80% | 60% |
| C (Decompensated) | 10–15 | 45% | 35% |

### MELD Score
MELD = 9.57 × ln(creatinine) + 3.78 × ln(bilirubin) + 11.20 × ln(INR) + 6.43
- Range: 6–40 (higher = worse prognosis)
- MELD >15: Liver transplantation listing typically recommended
- MELD >35: >80% 3-month mortality

### Major Complications of Cirrhosis

#### Ascites
- SAAG ≥1.1 g/dL: Portal hypertension cause (cirrhosis, right HF)
- Management: Sodium restriction (<2g/day), Spironolactone (100 mg/day) ± Furosemide (40 mg/day)
- Tense ascites: Large volume paracentesis; albumin 6–8 g/L removed

#### Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis (SBP)
- Diagnosis: Ascitic PMN ≥250 cells/mm³
- Treatment: Cefotaxime 2g IV q8h; add Albumin 1.5 g/kg day 1, 1 g/kg day 3 (reduces renal failure)
- Prophylaxis: Norfloxacin 400 mg daily (after 1st episode)

#### Hepatic Encephalopathy
- Grades I–IV (from personality change → coma)
- Management: Lactulose (titrate to 2–3 soft stools/day); Rifaximin 550 mg BD (reduces recurrence)
- Precipitants: GI bleeding, infection, sedatives, constipation, hyponatremia

#### Hepatorenal Syndrome (HRS)
- HRS-AKI: Creatinine ≥1.5 mg/dL, no response to volume expansion, no intrinsic renal disease
- Treatment: Terlipressin + Albumin (most evidence)

### Red Flags
- Ascites + fever + abdominal pain: SBP until diagnostic paracentesis proves otherwise
- Encephalopathy + GI bleeding: Double precipitant; urgent lactulose + treat bleed
- Acute-on-chronic liver failure (ACLF): Jaundice + coagulopathy + organ failure; MELD rapidly rising; urgent transplant evaluation
- New hepatic artery thrombosis post-transplant: Surgical emergency

## Febrile Neutropenia: Definition, Risk & Management

### Definition
- Fever: Single oral temperature ≥38.3°C OR ≥38.0°C sustained for ≥1 hour
- Neutropenia: ANC (Absolute Neutrophil Count) <500 cells/μL OR <1000 cells/μL with predicted nadir <500 cells/μL

### MASCC Risk Score (Low-risk criteria)
| Parameter | Points |
|-----------|--------|
| Symptoms: no or mild | 5 |
| No hypotension | 5 |
| No COPD | 4 |
| Solid tumor or hematologic malignancy without prior fungal infection | 4 |
| No dehydration | 3 |
| Outpatient status | 3 |
| Age <60 years | 2 |

MASCC ≥21: Low risk; outpatient oral antibiotics (Ciprofloxacin + Amoxicillin-clavulanate)
MASCC <21: High risk; IV antibiotics + admission

### Empirical IV Antibiotic Regimens
| Risk | Preferred Regimen |
|------|------------------|
| Low-risk inpatient | Cefepime 2g IV q8h OR Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h |
| High-risk (suspected Pseudomonas, unstable) | Cefepime OR Meropenem (anti-pseudomonal) |
| MRSA risk factors (skin/soft tissue source, hemodynamically unstable) | Add Vancomycin |
| Persistent fever >4–7 days or antifungal indication | Fluconazole OR Micafungin OR Amphotericin B |

### Duration of Antibiotics
- Continue until ANC >500 cells/μL AND patient afebrile ≥48h
- If ANC recovery delayed: Reassess at day 5–7; escalate antifungal coverage

### G-CSF (Filgrastim) Use
- Primary prophylaxis: If chemotherapy regimen carries >20% febrile neutropenia risk
- Therapeutic: Can shorten neutropenia duration; not routinely given during acute febrile neutropenia episode
- Dose: 5 mcg/kg SC daily until ANC >2000 cells/μL

### Red Flags
- ANC <100 cells/μL + fever >72h: Profound neutropenia; escalate to Meropenem + antifungal
- Hypotension + ANC <500: Neutropenic sepsis; treat as oncologic emergency with full sepsis protocol
- Perirectal abscess in neutropenia: Do NOT perform incision and drainage; Pip-Taz + anaerobic coverage
- New pulmonary infiltrate + neutropenia: CT chest; consider invasive aspergillosis (Voriconazole)

## Hypothyroidism: Classification, Diagnosis & Management

### Classification
| Type | TSH | Free T4 | Cause |
|------|-----|---------|-------|
| Primary | ↑ (>4.5 mIU/L) | ↓ | Hashimoto, post-ablation, iodine deficiency |
| Secondary | ↓ or Normal | ↓ | Pituitary failure |
| Tertiary | ↓ or Normal | ↓ | Hypothalamic failure |
| Subclinical | ↑ (4.5–10 mIU/L) | Normal | Early/mild primary hypothyroidism |

### Clinical Features
| System | Symptoms |
|--------|----------|
| General | Fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance |
| Cardiovascular | Bradycardia, pericardial effusion, diastolic hypertension |
| Neurological | Slowed reflexes (delayed relaxation phase), cognitive slowing |
| Musculoskeletal | Myalgia, proximal myopathy, elevated CK |
| Dermatological | Dry skin, hair loss (outer 1/3 of eyebrows), myxedema |
| Reproductive | Menorrhagia, infertility, hyperprolactinemia |
| Metabolic | Hypercholesterolemia, hyponatremia, macrocytic anemia |

### Diagnosis
- 1st line: TSH
- If TSH elevated: Add Free T4
- If TSH low + hypothyroid symptoms: Add Free T3, pituitary MRI
- Anti-TPO antibodies: Confirms Hashimoto's thyroiditis

### Treatment
| Indication | Starting Levothyroxine Dose |
|------------|----------------------------|
| Healthy adult | 1.6 mcg/kg/day PO |
| Elderly or cardiac disease | 12.5–25 mcg/day; titrate slowly |
| Pregnancy | Increase dose by 20–30% immediately on confirmation |
| Subclinical hypothyroidism | Treat if TSH >10, symptoms, or pregnancy planning |

### Myxedema Coma (Severe/Emergency)
- Precipitants: Infection, cold exposure, medications (sedatives, anesthesia)
- Features: Hypothermia (<35°C), bradycardia, altered consciousness, hypoventilation, hyponatremia
- Management:
  - T4: 200–400 mcg IV loading → 100 mcg IV daily
  - T3: 5–20 mcg IV bolus (faster CNS effect)
  - Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV (before thyroid hormone — exclude concurrent adrenal insufficiency)
  - Passive rewarming; mechanical ventilation if needed

### Red Flags
- TSH >100 mIU/L: Severe primary hypothyroidism; risk of myxedema
- Hyponatremia <125 mEq/L in hypothyroidism: SIADH-like picture; levothyroxine corrects it
- New angina after starting levothyroxine: Demand ischemia from increased metabolic rate; slow dose titration
- Pregnancy + elevated TSH: Start/increase levothyroxine immediately; TSH target <2.5 mIU/L in 1st trimester

## Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD): KDIGO Classification & Management

### Definition
Abnormalities of kidney structure or function present >3 months with health implications.

### KDIGO CKD Classification
| Stage | GFR (mL/min/1.73m²) | Description |
|-------|---------------------|-------------|
| G1 | ≥90 | Normal or high (with markers of kidney damage) |
| G2 | 60–89 | Mildly decreased |
| G3a | 45–59 | Mildly-moderately decreased |
| G3b | 30–44 | Moderately-severely decreased |
| G4 | 15–29 | Severely decreased |
| G5 | <15 | Kidney failure |

### Albuminuria Categories (A1–A3)
| Category | ACR (mg/g) | Description |
|----------|------------|-------------|
| A1 | <30 | Normal to mildly increased |
| A2 | 30–300 | Moderately increased |
| A3 | >300 | Severely increased |

### Risk Stratification Matrix (GFR × Albuminuria)
High-risk CKD: G3b–G5 OR A3 (any stage).

### Management by Stage
| Focus | Intervention |
|-------|-------------|
| BP target | <130/80 mmHg (ACEi/ARB preferred if proteinuria) |
| Proteinuria reduction | ACEi or ARB (dual blockade contraindicated) |
| Glycemic control | HbA1c <7% in DM |
| Cardiovascular risk | Statin (all CKD patients with CVD risk) |
| Anemia | Iron (target ferritin 200–500, TSAT 20–50%); Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents if Hb <10 g/dL |
| Bone disease (CKD-MBD) | Phosphate restriction; Vitamin D; Calcimimetics (CKD G5D) |
| Acidosis | Sodium bicarbonate if HCO₃⁻ <22 mEq/L |
| SGLT2 inhibitors | Dapagliflozin/Empagliflozin: Reduce CKD progression (GFR ≥20) |

### Indications for Renal Replacement Therapy
- GFR <10–15 mL/min with uremic symptoms
- Refractory fluid overload, acidosis, hyperkalemia
- Pericarditis, encephalopathy, bleeding diathesis (uremic)

### Red Flags
- Sudden eGFR decline >25%: Check for AKI on CKD (NSAIDs, contrast, dehydration, ACEi)
- Hyperkalemia >6.0 mEq/L: Emergency; cardiac monitoring; treat acutely
- GFR <30 + metformin: Risk of lactic acidosis; reduce/discontinue metformin
- Unexplained rapid GFR loss: Biopsy for RPGN (ANCA, anti-GBM), multiple myeloma cast nephropathy

## Aortic Dissection: Classification, Diagnosis & Management

### Definition
Tear in aortic intima allowing blood to enter medial layer, creating true and false lumens. Medical/surgical emergency.

### Stanford Classification
| Type | Involvement | Mortality Without Treatment | Management |
|------|-------------|----------------------------|------------|
| Type A | Ascending aorta (regardless of origin) | 1–2% per hour initially | Emergency surgical repair |
| Type B | Descending aorta only (distal to left subclavian) | Lower (untreated) | Medical (antihypertensive) unless complicated |

### Clinical Presentation
- Abrupt tearing/ripping pain: Anterior chest (ascending), Back/interscapular (descending)
- Pulse deficit: >20 mmHg BP differential between arms
- Hypertension (most common) OR hypotension (cardiac tamponade, aortic rupture)
- Neurological deficits: Carotid involvement → stroke; spinal artery → paraplegia
- New AR murmur: Aortic valve ring dilation

### Diagnostic Algorithm
1. ECG: Rule out STEMI (if dissection mimics MI, thrombolytics are catastrophic)
2. CXR: Widened mediastinum (>8 cm), pleural effusion
3. D-dimer: >500 ng/mL sensitivity >95%; negative D-dimer + low pretest probability: Reasonable to exclude
4. CT aortography (gold standard): Identifies extent, entry tear, branch vessel involvement
5. TEE: Bedside, useful in hemodynamically unstable patients

### Medical Management (Type B)
- Target: SBP 100–120 mmHg AND HR <60 bpm
- First line: IV Labetalol OR Esmolol (beta-blockade first, then vasodilate)
- If beta-blocker contraindicated: Diltiazem IV
- Avoid pure vasodilators (Nitroprusside) without beta-blocker (reflex tachycardia increases aortic wall stress)

### Type B Complicated Indications for Intervention (TEVAR)
- Malperfusion (limb, mesenteric, renal)
- Aortic expansion >5 mm in 6 months OR diameter >6 cm
- Refractory pain or hypertension despite maximal medical therapy
- Rupture

### Red Flags
- Hypotension + Type A dissection: Cardiac tamponade or rupture; immediate OR
- Dissection + inferior STEMI on ECG: Right coronary artery ostium involvement; surgery preferred over PCI
- Dissection extending to mesenteric artery: Bowel ischemia; surgical emergency
- Anticoagulation or thrombolytics given for suspected STEMI actually Type A: Catastrophic; check for asymmetric pulses before thrombolytics

## Kawasaki Disease: Diagnostic Criteria & Management

### Definition
Kawasaki disease (KD): Acute self-limiting vasculitis primarily affecting medium-sized arteries. Most common acquired heart disease in children in developed countries. Peak age: 6 months–5 years.

### Diagnostic Criteria (Classic KD)
Fever ≥5 days PLUS ≥4 of the following 5 criteria:
| Criterion | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| **C** - Conjunctivitis | Bilateral, non-purulent bulbar conjunctival injection |
| **R** - Rash | Polymorphous (not vesicular or bullous) |
| **A** - Adenopathy | Cervical lymphadenopathy >1.5 cm; usually unilateral, non-suppurative |
| **S** - Strawberry tongue/lips | Erythema and cracking of lips, strawberry tongue, pharyngeal erythema |
| **H** - Hands/Feet | Acute: Erythema + edema; Subacute: Periungual desquamation (Harlequin sign) |

### Incomplete KD
- Fever ≥5 days + 2–3 clinical features
- If laboratory markers positive (CRP ≥3 mg/dL OR ESR ≥40 mm/h) + echo or lab criteria: Treat as KD

### Laboratory Findings
| Marker | Expected Finding |
|--------|----------------|
| CRP | Elevated (>3 mg/dL) |
| ESR | Elevated (>40 mm/h) |
| WBC | Leukocytosis (>15,000/μL in acute phase) |
| Albumin | ≤3 g/dL |
| ALT | Mildly elevated (not specific) |
| Platelets | Thrombocytosis in week 2–3 (often >1,000,000/μL) |
| Urine | Sterile pyuria (urethral inflammation) |
| Na+ | Hyponatremia |

### Treatment
1. **IVIG**: 2 g/kg IV single dose (within first 10 days; reduces coronary aneurysm risk from 25% to <4%)
2. **Aspirin**: High-dose 30–50 mg/kg/day until afebrile, then low-dose 3–5 mg/kg/day for 6–8 weeks
3. **Corticosteroids**: For IVIG-resistant KD (2nd IVIG dose OR Infliximab OR Methylprednisolone)
4. **Echocardiography**: At diagnosis, 2 weeks, 6–8 weeks

### Red Flags
- Coronary artery aneurysm (Z-score ≥2.5): Highest risk if >8 mm (giant aneurysm); anticoagulation + clopidogrel
- IVIG resistance (persistent fever ≥36h): Second IVIG dose; consider Infliximab or steroids
- Kawasaki shock syndrome (hypotension): Aggressive IVIG + steroids; ICU
- Hydrops of gallbladder: Common in acute KD; self-limiting; surgical intervention rarely needed

## Septic Arthritis: Diagnosis & Management

### Definition
Joint space infection, typically bacterial. Medical emergency requiring joint drainage and antibiotics to prevent irreversible cartilage destruction.

### Risk Factors
- Prosthetic joint
- Pre-existing joint disease (RA, gout, OA)
- IV drug use
- Immunocompromised state
- Recent joint surgery or injection

### Synovial Fluid Analysis
| Type | WBC/μL | PMN% | Glucose | Appearance |
|------|--------|------|---------|------------|
| Normal | <200 | <25% | Normal | Clear, yellow |
| Non-inflammatory | 200–2000 | <25% | Normal | Yellow, clear |
| Inflammatory (RA, gout) | 2000–50,000 | 50–75% | Slightly low | Turbid |
| Septic | >50,000 (often >100,000) | >90% | Very low | Opaque, purulent |
| Crystal (gout/pseudogout) | 2000–50,000 | Variable | Normal | Turbid |

### Crystal Analysis
| Crystal | Appearance | Joint | Diagnosis |
|---------|------------|-------|-----------|
| Monosodium urate | Needle-shaped, negatively birefringent | MTP, ankle, knee | Gout |
| Calcium pyrophosphate | Rhomboid, positively birefringent | Knee, wrist | Pseudogout (CPPD) |

### Causative Organisms (by Population)
| Population | Likely Pathogen |
|------------|-----------------|
| Adults | S. aureus (most common), Streptococcus |
| Sexually active young adults | N. gonorrhoeae |
| Children <2 years | S. aureus, Streptococcus, K. kingae |
| IV drug users | P. aeruginosa, S. aureus |
| Prosthetic joint | S. epidermidis, S. aureus, Cutibacterium acnes |

### Management
1. Arthrocentesis (diagnostic AND therapeutic) — drain completely
2. Empirical antibiotics:
   - Non-gonococcal: Flucloxacillin 2g IV q6h (MRSA risk: Vancomycin)
   - Gonococcal: Ceftriaxone 1g IV daily
3. Repeat daily aspiration OR surgical washout if: loculated, hip/shoulder joint, poor response
4. IV antibiotics → PO transition when CRP falling and clinically improved (typically 2 weeks IV)

### Red Flags
- WBC >100,000/μL: Septic arthritis until proven otherwise; do NOT delay drainage
- Septic arthritis of hip in child: Surgical drainage (irreversible AVN risk within hours)
- Fever + hot joint in RA on DMARDs: Septic arthritis vs. flare; WBC >50,000 favors infection
- Negative Gram stain and culture: Does NOT rule out septic arthritis; treat if WBC >50,000

## ARDS: Berlin Criteria & Lung-Protective Ventilation

### Berlin Definition (2012)
| Parameter | Criteria |
|-----------|----------|
| Timing | Within 1 week of known clinical insult or new/worsening respiratory symptoms |
| Imaging | Bilateral opacities on CXR/CT; not fully explained by effusions, atelectasis, or nodules |
| Origin of edema | Not fully explained by cardiac failure or fluid overload (BNP, echo if unclear) |
| Oxygenation | PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio on PEEP ≥5 cmH₂O |

### Severity Classification
| Category | PaO₂/FiO₂ (mmHg) | PEEP |
|----------|------------------|------|
| Mild | 201–300 | ≥5 cmH₂O |
| Moderate | 101–200 | ≥5 cmH₂O |
| Severe | ≤100 | ≥5 cmH₂O |

### Lung-Protective Ventilation (ARDSnet Protocol)
| Parameter | Setting |
|-----------|--------|
| Tidal Volume | 6 mL/kg IBW (NOT actual body weight) |
| Plateau Pressure | ≤30 cmH₂O |
| Driving Pressure | ≤15 cmH₂O (Plateau − PEEP) |
| PEEP | Titrated to FiO₂ requirements (ARDSnet PEEP/FiO₂ table) |
| Target SpO₂ | 88–95% (permissive hypoxemia acceptable) |
| Target PaO₂ | 55–80 mmHg |
| pH target | 7.25–7.45 (permissive hypercapnia acceptable if pH ≥7.20) |
| FiO₂ | Titrate to minimum to achieve SpO₂ target |

### Adjunct Therapies for Severe ARDS
| Intervention | Evidence | Indication |
|-------------|----------|------------|
| Prone positioning | Reduces 28-day mortality (PROSEVA) | PaO₂/FiO₂ ≤150; ≥16h/day sessions |
| Neuromuscular blockade | Reduces effort breathing, improves synchrony | Moderate-severe within 48h |
| High PEEP strategy | Improves oxygenation; no mortality benefit shown | Moderate-severe ARDS |
| Inhaled Nitric Oxide | Improves oxygenation transiently | Bridge to ECMO consideration |
| ECMO (VV-ECMO) | Last resort; centers with expertise | Severe ARDS + PaO₂/FiO₂ <80 despite optimal ventilation |

### Red Flags
- Plateau pressure >30 cmH₂O: Barotrauma risk; reduce tidal volume
- Driving pressure >15 cmH₂O: Associated with increased mortality; modify PEEP or VT
- PaO₂/FiO₂ <80 on 100% FiO₂ + PEEP 15–20: ECMO consideration
- Prone positioning contraindications: Spinal instability, open abdomen, increased ICP

## ACE Inhibitors: Mechanism, Indications & Adverse Effects

### Mechanism of Action
ACE inhibitors block Angiotensin Converting Enzyme → prevent Angiotensin I → Angiotensin II conversion:
- Vasodilation: ↓ Angiotensin II → ↓ vasoconstriction + ↓ aldosterone → ↓ sodium/water retention
- Bradykinin accumulation: Prevents bradykinin degradation → additional vasodilation + cough (adverse effect)
- Renal: ↓ Efferent arteriolar constriction → ↓ intraglomerular pressure → proteinuria reduction

### Drug Examples & Dosing
| Drug | Prodrug | Starting Dose | Max Dose |
|------|---------|--------------|----------|
| Ramipril | Yes (Ramiprilat) | 2.5 mg OD | 10 mg OD |
| Lisinopril | No | 5 mg OD | 40 mg OD |
| Enalapril | Yes (Enalaprilat) | 5 mg BD | 40 mg/day |
| Captopril | No | 6.25 mg TID | 150 mg/day |
| Perindopril | Yes | 2–4 mg OD | 8 mg OD |

### Clinical Indications
- Hypertension (first-line in diabetic nephropathy, proteinuric CKD)
- Heart failure (HFrEF — proven mortality benefit)
- Post-MI (within 24h; reduces LV remodeling)
- Diabetic nephropathy (reduces progression; standard of care)
- Chronic CKD with proteinuria
- Secondary stroke prevention (HOPE trial: Ramipril)

### Adverse Effects
| Effect | Mechanism | Frequency | Management |
|--------|-----------|-----------|------------|
| Dry cough | Bradykinin accumulation | 10–20% | Switch to ARB |
| Hyperkalemia | ↓ Aldosterone | 1–10% | Monitor K⁺; avoid if K⁺ >5.0 mEq/L |
| Angioedema | Bradykinin (not IgE) | 0.1–0.5% | Discontinue immediately; icatibant for acute |
| AKI (functional) | Reduced GFR in stenosis | Variable | Discontinue; bilateral RAS contraindication |
| Hypotension | Vasodilation | Common early | Start low; monitor first dose |
| Teratogenicity | Fetal RAAS required for development | — | Contraindicated in pregnancy (2nd/3rd trimester) |

### Red Flags
- Angioedema with lip/tongue/larynx: Airway emergency; epinephrine + stop ACEi; bradykinin-mediated (antihistamines less effective)
- Creatinine rise >30% after ACEi start: Possible bilateral renal artery stenosis; stop and investigate
- ACEi + NSAID + diuretic: Triple whammy; high AKI risk
- ACEi + ARB dual blockade: ONTARGET trial; increased renal failure and hypotension without added benefit

## Sickle Cell Disease: Complications & Management

### Pathophysiology
Homozygous HbS: Point mutation (Glu→Val at β-globin position 6) → HbS polymerization under hypoxia → RBC sickling → vaso-occlusion, hemolysis, end-organ damage.

### Major Acute Complications

#### Vaso-Occlusive Crisis (VOC)
- Presentation: Severe bone pain (long bones, spine, ribs); triggered by cold, dehydration, infection, hypoxia
- Management:
  1. IV fluids (maintain euvolemia)
  2. Analgesia: WHO pain ladder — opioids (Morphine PCA for severe)
  3. Oxygen only if SpO2 <95%
  4. NSAIDs (adjunct unless renal impairment)

#### Acute Chest Syndrome (ACS)
- Definition: New pulmonary infiltrate + fever AND one of: chest pain, cough, dyspnea, hypoxia
- Mechanism: Fat embolism, infection, in-situ sickling in pulmonary vasculature
- Management:
  1. Incentive spirometry (prevents atelectasis)
  2. Empirical antibiotics (Ceftriaxone + Azithromycin — atypical cover)
  3. Simple transfusion (raise Hb to 10 g/dL if <9 g/dL)
  4. Exchange transfusion if rapid deterioration or PaO₂ <70 mmHg
  5. Oxygen; mechanical ventilation if respiratory failure

#### Aplastic Crisis
- Cause: Parvovirus B19 infection → temporary erythroid suppression
- Hb drops dramatically; reticulocytes ↓
- Treatment: Transfusion support; self-limiting (10–14 days)

#### Stroke in SCD
- Children: Highest stroke risk of any pediatric condition
- Prevention: Transcranial Doppler (TCD) screening annually age 2–16; exchange transfusion if TCD velocity >200 cm/s
- Acute stroke: Emergency exchange transfusion to reduce HbS <30%

### Disease-Modifying Treatment
| Intervention | Mechanism | Benefit |
|-------------|-----------|--------|
| Hydroxyurea | ↑ HbF production (inhibits sickling) | Reduces VOC frequency, ACS, mortality |
| Crizanlizumab | Anti-P-selectin antibody | Reduces VOC frequency |
| Voxelotor | Increases HbS oxygen affinity | Reduces sickling, improves Hb |
| L-glutamine | Reduces oxidative stress | Reduces ACS |
| Curative: HSCT | Allogenic hematopoietic stem cell transplant | Cure if matched sibling donor |

### Red Flags
- Priapism >4h: Urological emergency; aspiration + irrigation ± exchange transfusion
- Acute splenic sequestration: Sudden Hb drop + splenomegaly (children <5 years); urgent transfusion
- ACS + deteriorating SpO2: Exchange transfusion; ICU ventilatory support
- Stroke: Reduce HbS to <30% via exchange transfusion urgently
